lines.x: 'Terra', 'the land', 'these lands', the nations 'across the world'.
These terms and the language around them flow in widespread use only because of how narrow they actually are.

Has there ever been some majestic language of the ancient past that could describe everything between heaven and earth? Where we put into words the 'everything' we might encounter in our day-to-day lives?
A grandiose term encompassing the earth, the sky, and even the seas of which the landbound nations know so little? A single word to narrate millennia of life's progress?
Where among the collective mind of mankind is such a word, Kal'tsit?
I'm sure you know the answer yourself.
I know quite a bit myself, yet still I find myself asking, day after day. No man may truly escape the unknown, and the unknown will forever torment mankind.
(Ancient Sarkaz language) 'Alma.' 'World.'
Hm. And here I thought you would go with the Sargonian word 'Alam'. The same idea, I think.
Yours was a Sarkaz language?
It was.
Strange. You're a Feline who calls herself a doctor, yet your first instinct is to use the devils' tongue.
Your identity is like the ever-changing clouds above the sea. Never content to remain the same.
Language will not change its essence for such trifles.
Language, hmm... Now look here, Kal'tsit. A well.
Do you know how to draw water from it? Do you know how the Iberians used these blue cornerstones to build their houses?
If the Iberian nobility hadn't let their pride go to their heads, the ocean would surely have made the nation an impenetrable fortress.
The well is deep, and we're very close to the shore.
Look. It hasn't yet dried up. There is a false sun inside.
We should be on our way, sir.
The elderly man does not answer her. He simply picks up a small rock in silence.

The rock could not be more commonplace, with no particular reason to fiddle with. Yet, as though absorbed in the sensation of the sea breeze on his skin, he continues to gaze at the bottom of the well.
Kal'tsit doesn't push the issue. She silently waits for the man in front of her to answer. She sees that the clouds are moving. The winds are strong.
There is no rush, Kal'tsit. There is no rush. We have ample time for rest... no matter the arrangements you've made.
If I dropped this rock into the well, how long would it take for us to hear the splash?
About five to six seconds.
Even the most simple Iberian knows how to make use of the sea. Knowledge that went into this deep coastal well.
And yet, a Victorian farmer or Columbian worker might not even know what the vast sea looks like.
Hmm... Kal'tsit, how many questions have I asked you on our journey so far?
The old man holds his arm out, the rock still in his palm. Then he lets go, sending it tumbling downward.
Despite its rapid descent, the two of them lock their eyes onto it. Yet without adequate sunlight, they soon find themselves unable to follow its trek into the depths.
It has been two, perhaps three seconds. Nothing but silence shrouds the two of them. Even the wind has come to a halt.
How quiet, Kal'tsit thinks to herself. How quiet indeed.
*Plop*
The quiet sound echoes through the well's walls, as though reminding them that, in that brief moment lasting but a few seconds, all the lands are still.
One hundred twenty-three. Quite a bewitching number.
One hundred twenty-three questions, over one hundred twenty-three years. The Inquisition boasts not a single Inquisitor older than me.
Most have died in battle, a few of old age, and even fewer gave in to cowardice during their endless ordeal. It did not end very well for them.
I've experienced all there is of Iberia. I've seen its fleets setting sail, and heard the Victorian envoys quivering.
It was a rude awakening when the Profound Silence destroyed our nation. My countrymen are still drowning in their hatred for the disaster, and confused over the loss of their dream.
Liberi don't live long lives. Even the Liberi Elders, the oldest and healthiest of them, are unable to shoulder Iberia's present calamity.
Your age is a miracle. One forged by the Inquisition, and which also grants you an unprecedented sense of devotion.
In that era, when both glory and ideology began to crumble, you and the earliest Inquisition members were revered as 'Saints.'
'Saints.' A title for those who once served as a guiding beacon for mankind. But just like firewood, it's become nothing but ashes.

I've watched the seasons cycle by a full hundred and twenty-three times, yet it seems only the cold winters have left their mark on me. I've seen so many truths with my own eyes, yet can never recall the sight of spring.
Kal'tsit...
Yes?
The look in the old man's eyes changes.
Faith is a language, and prayers can pave roads. At this very moment, the nation of Iberia, or perhaps an existence beyond that, is caressing its chin, examining the wind speed.
So far, I've lived one hundred twenty-three years. I've asked you one hundred twenty-three questions, based on my personal experiences.
Nonetheless, you answered each and every one. You know everything that I know.
Man is making progress, and new secrets will forever emerge, yet our knowledge will never be able to overcome that wall we call 'the known.'
Regardless, the enemy we face belongs to the other side of that wall, in the dark forest that lies beyond.
And you, Kal'tsit. Do you belong to our side of the wall... or to the depths of that forest?
That is up to you.
Hoh... You might even know the number of leaves on its trees. What a terrifying woman.
Make no mistake, the saints of Iberia remain the pinnacle that humanity can reach. Yet you've exceeded that, marking you as inhuman.
Perhaps you've extended your life with special Arts, perhaps you've inherited some kind of ancient identity, or perhaps you possess a soul that had great ambitions.

Even if our faith is only a lie built atop ideology, we nevertheless once held onto it firmly. What is truly saddening, though, is that when old age finally bests us all, we will have not yet managed to solve our problems.
While those of us who have bested old age—via Originium Arts or other means—will only come to fear endless suffering at the hands of our enemies.
When did Ægir become such an unknown terror to Iberia?
How could an unarmed child so easily accept the help of a stranger brandishing a weapon?
What if that child were about to drown?
Then we would still require proof. Is Ægir's current situation really as you say? Can the Seaborn and their source truly be defeated?
If you cannot back up these claims, then Iberia will not trust you. I will not trust you.
Before I die, you... you must all prove it to Iberia.
Otherwise, the seawater will quench the flame of civilization. Before the other nations can prepare, they will swarm past Iberia... and tear this 'world' apart.
Perhaps this too—the refutation of Terran civilization—is itself a part of their survival-of-the-fittest mindset.
Or perhaps there is nothing inherently different between us and them.
A seaside town, huh? Relaxed, but gloomy. Quiet, but bustling. A lively place, but with danger and mystery around every corner.
Just the place to get us in that creative groove!
What do you girls say?
(Riffs lightheartedly)
The shore's got an unsettling vibe. I can already smell all that stuff I'd rather not.
I really don't think we should be visiting the waterfront so carelessly.
Does it matter, if we can find inspiration? C'mon, look how fired up about it Dan is.
(Riffs depressingly)
Time to find our muse!
Right... It's a good chance.
I can't believe we're actually looking to that messed up ocean for inspiration now.
Yeah, which is exactly why it's a fresh new vibe!
Took the words outta my mouth.
(Riffs in agreement)
Well, should we split up, then? I can take our luggage to the hotel on my own.
Alright! I'll be checking stuff out over there.
All by yourself?
I'm glad SOMEONE's worrying. Just don't forget we're playing with fire here.
So where's the hotel?
That reminds me, how did a small town like this get itself a hotel anyway? Do people even come here?
They need to make a living somehow.
Right. No living, no music.
(Riffs)
The sea...
We're back.
Excuse me... can I get past?
Oh, sure, no problem.
Have you been sitting in the chapel again, Mr... uhh...?
Elysium.
Sorry... We've even run into each other several times already.
Don't worry about it. The name 'Elysium' isn't very common here.
Actually, someone as smoking hot as me has gotta be a rare sight in Iberia to begin with, right?
Uhh...
I don't know about that... but someone as confident as you, maybe.
Right...
It took spending some time here to realize that the place that I always called home doesn't really feel like home.
Are you still waiting for someone here, Mr. Elysium? It's already been several days, hasn't it?
Haha, not like I have a choice. That's just how things are in my line of work. Besides, I'd rather hang out in comfort here than that in primeval forest infested with bugs and Originium.
You're from outside then?
Oh, you can tell? Is it because of the vibe I give off?
Actually... uh... I guess you could say that?
What do you mean?
Like... the way you dress?
You know your stuff.
Gran Faro doesn't see a lot of visitors... There's not a lot of folks here, so everyone in town knows everyone else.
Besides... What with how close to the sea we are, not a lot of Liberi are willing to talk to Ægir these days.
Especially after the Inquisition started showing up more often.
Right...
I get you. I've been through similar stuff plenty of times.
Does this happen in other Iberian cities, too? Like in those prosperous ones I hear about in the rumors?
Not just in Iberia, kid. The way I see it, it's pretty much the same no matter where you go. Life's tough everywhere.
But I gotta say, some of the Ægirs I've met are a real handful. Just thinking about 'em gives me a headache. Wish I could beam all my common sense straight into their brains.
Which makes you pretty easy to get along with, by comparison.
Uhh...
Okay, now THAT's a face asking who'd ever want the common sense of a guy like me.
N-No it's not!
Sure, I look like an unemployed bum lazing about here 24-7, but I've actually come a loooong way and been through waaaaay too much.
So... you're an explorer, then? What's life like for the Ægir in other places?
Haha...
Not too bad.
Not too bad...
Mayor Thiago... You needn't come here every day.
I've been hearing some bad rumors lately. I'm worried about you guys, Amaia.
Bad rumors... You mean about the cultists?
But we're not just close to the sea, we're close to the Inquisition too, and the very heart of Iberia.
So how could there possibly be cultists here?
Who knows? Happens all the time in coastal towns though. Hardly peculiar at all.
Also... I heard someone saw a monster on the shore.
Did Old Pedri tell you that?
You know the kind of nonsense he goes on about when he hasn't had his drink.
You're a kind man, Thiago. I'm sure you're just worried about Jordi, the boy who works at the chapel.
I... I'm not a very sophisticated one though. Maybe you can put me at ease... help me sleep better at night.
These rumors are just rumors, right? The ghost ship, the cultists, the sea monsters?
The Inquisition hasn't let us go out to sea in a long, long time.
Right. Can't even remember how long... Eighty-four years? Eighty-five?
Try fifty or sixty. The Profound Silence happened in 1038.
Have you really been trapped on land for so long, in your memories? You aren't even that old. Have you been a prisoner of this place since before you were born?
If living in poverty counts as imprisonment, then yes, I've been a prisoner for a long, long time.
I haven't so much as looked at the sea since Marin died. I loathe it.
But you're not worried about the gossip, are you? You're worried about the Inquisition. And even more so about the Penal Battalion coming here to take away all the Ægirs.
*Sigh*, poor Thiago...
The Inquisition snuffed out the love of your life, an Ægir woman. Your anger and resentment is perfectly understandable, but be careful what you say. For everyone's sake.
...Maybe you're right.
Okay, I'll be careful. You always know more than the rest of us, anyway. So what've you been up to lately?
The same old. Doing my job as a third-rate translator in this frighteningly laidback seaside town.
That said, it's been months since a Messenger stopped by. All my drafts have been piling up, and it's giving me a headache.
*Sigh*... Translation! I've never even set foot outside this small town my whole life. Anything besides Iberian may as well be gibberish to me.
Hm, it's getting late.
Right, Jordi should be off work soon. I only hope that the Inquisition isn't gonna give the lad any trouble over that gossip, these next few days.
I'll see you later.
Be safe, Mayor Thiago.
...Cultists. Hm.
The young woman named Amaia ponders for a moment before turning her mind back to the papers, as always.
Ursine novels, Kazimierzian biographies, Leithanian poetry... even Sargonian folk tales.
All the lands of Terra lie before her as a thick pile of literature, each word waiting to be deciphered in a solemn, orderly manner.
The air is extraordinarily humid. She can't help but pinch the corner of the page.
The sensation of the sea...
'The waves continue to accelerate, and the winds are frightened. The corals line the sky, painting it a neon shade.'
'We will come to detest ourselves for not having wings.'
Are you finished with your work?
Ah, uh... yes.
As you can see, the chapel doesn't see a lot of visitors. I mostly just keep the place tidy.
Are you still waiting today?
Yeah. They should've been here by last week, but my superior did warn me that they're a weird bunch. So I guess being late is normal enough.
Still, it's hard to call that a good thing. But why would anyone choose to meet up in Gran Faro of all places?
Strange, right? I thought so, too. That's why I volunteered for the job when Misery brought it up.
Not that I expected to waste a whole month here. That's a hell of a long time to be on standby.
But... normally the Inquisition wouldn't have let outsiders like you in at all.
Because of how close it is to their command center?
Or... because it's close to the sea?
Maybe both.
I see...
So how did you make it here? I haven't traveled much, but even I can tell that Gran Faro is a backwater...
If you really wanna know, I could use someone to chat with. I'm dying of boredom over here.
Then if it's not too much trouble— Oh!
Sorry, I didn't realize it was so late. Uncle Thiago is waiting. I should head back to make dinner.
Family?
Uh... Something like that. He's the one who raised me.
Look, you don't have to be such a doormat just because you're an Ægir. When someone's kind to you, try to be more positive.
Uh, thanks... Mr. Elysium.
Gran Faro. It means 'the great lighthouse' in an ancient Iberian tongue.
Lighthouses once shone upon ships both large and small, and served as homes for all seamen to return to.
Many souls dwelled beneath the reefs, until the ships fell in love with the ocean, accepting its embrace and facing their own destruction.
(Amaia? Where is she going... Hm?)
(Some sort of commotion up ahead? That's unusual. What's going on?)
Where are you heading?
The chapel! Peeps here shouldn't be using their hands to pray, they should learn to drum instead!
This square is the center of the town. Everything's built around it.
A sculpture of a lighthouse.
A guiding landmark to the sea... and the town.
What a lame analogy!
What a bunch of weirdos. Let's not get too close.
Didn't Old Pedri mention something like this before?
Don't get involved, lest the Inquisition take you away.
Sheesh, why are there all these outsiders lately? The Inquisition just doesn't care anymore, huh?
(I haven't seen any of them before... Look at the way they're dressed... Are they musicians?)
(Er, is she looking at me?)
H-Hello?
In the past, I wouldn't have batted an eye at seeing an Ægir near the coast, but it sure is a rare sight these days.
Do you know what's going on here?
W-What?
Oh, you wouldn't, huh? You aren't a child of the sea. You're land-born.
*Sigh*... Not that that's much of a surprise. Jeez.
Aya, over here!
Coming!
Oh, right... you... hmm...
I-Is something the matter?
Do you know what that sculpture is of? And do all the locals know?
Yes... It's a lighthouse. Apparently, they needed a lot of people to work on it way back when, and many of the workers' families ended up staying here.
Like yours?
Uh... Yes. I still keep some relics from that era back home. Like, blueprints and pictures.
Yep... lighthouses. Iberian lighthouses. In a country on the land. That's kinda funny.
Anyway, the scent doesn't seem to be coming from you. Alright, then, you be extra careful.
I've never seen those people... Are those instruments?
Maybe they're the ones Mr. Elysium has been waiting for?
Jordi.
Oh, Uncle Thiago. Sorry. I should've come back sooner—
Don't you get involved with them. I didn't see ears OR tails, and no feathers either. They might be Ægirs.
I—
Jordi, my boy. You know I don't have anything against the Ægir, but remember what everyone's been saying lately.
Don't get too involved with these outsiders. If you see an Ægir near the sea and there's no one from the Inquisition or Penal Battalion around, then they gotta be trouble.
I don't think they're bad people, but the Inquisition might not see things the same way, so... I'm sorry. I just don't want to see them take you away. Be a good boy and listen.
I get it. You don't have to apologize.
I get it...
I didn't expect you to agree so quickly.
I know of the events in Sal Viento, with the three Abyssal Hunters. Irene always writes the most detailed reports, after all.
And above all, I know that if you meant Iberia harm, you had other, much simpler options.
The Abyssal Hunters... Suppose they are as strong and swift as the report suggests. The three of them are still controllable by the Inquisition.
We retain the authority to sentence them to death. The only reason they are still allowed to walk Iberian lands is thanks to my curiosity towards your so-called truth.
I can't help but wonder if Iberia's current predicament is a result of its pride, then.
The truth is that no one knows pride better than us. Pride is like the shadow always lurking behind every man.
The two inquisitors with whom you're acquainted have already received orders. Should the need arise, they will appear before us.
It seems we have a few unidentified Ægirs in Gran Faro as well. You never mentioned them.
Who are they?
They aren't Ægir.
Iberia is rarely mentioned in the historical record, but that doesn’t mean our history is short. At the height of our prosperity, we crossed the entire continent, advancing troops to even Bolívar.

Both coin and knowledge flooded into our cities like streams pouring into a valley from a high cliff. Those glorious waterfalls roared so deafeningly loud that they could overcome even the waves of the sea.
(Sargonian) Those beings that arose... or rather, the 'Feranmut.'
Perhaps it is them that I should keep an eye on. They show no signs of hostility, yet they keep me on edge... Maybe they once belonged to the sea.
Kal'tsit, you are the doctor here. Could you do the Inquisition a favor?
...Ask away.
The Inquisition's command center is very close to the coastline. We are prepared to face destruction at any moment.
But I need you, an outsider, to be our eyes, gazing forward.
Seeing as we're in the same boat, I have no reason to refuse.
Please, Doctor, spare me the mysteriousness your long life has earned you. Show us where the evil that lurks within us sleeps.
I'll take that as a compliment... and it would be my honor.
Gladiia.
Our brief time together has you addressing me differently. On that point alone, you are quite different from before.
Captain, I have a question.
We've already shared all the information we have, in the time since we left that reeking coast.
Not all of it.
We alone are more than enough. I don't understand why we must also bring—
Look at her now. This is how she once was.
I looked into all her treatment records at Rhodes Island... You've never seen her this quiet before either, have you?
Don't you want to help her?
And this will help?
Perhaps.
She alone can defeat her illusions. She is her own key. She can break through the results of those filthy experiments. She's Laurentina, after all.
But until then, we need to prepare a warm bed for her.
It was only for a short while, but she woke up... Even so, I stared at her like that.
Don't be so sentimental. We aren't her. If she doesn't mind the way she is, we have no right to meddle in it.
You trust Dr. Kal'tsit more than I thought you would.
Maybe. She willingly stayed in Iberia and created this opportunity for us. I was surprised. And I have to thank her.
I can only hope it all goes well for her.
Look at the time.
Guess it's not happening today either. And here I thought I'd get to see them sooner or later just by waiting in the most conspicuous building in town.
This really is such an uneventful place, though. If I'd known it'd be so boring here, I would've brought along a few more books from Rhodes Island—
—A Lateran chapel! And in such a great shape, even right next to the sea! Unbelievable! This place's style really has a different vibe, though.
I mean, as long as there are Laterans in chapels, we can jam whenever, but this place just feels so gloomy... Hm?
Ahh—
—Alty?!
You'd best stay away from the sea, Jordi. The chapel is clean and safe. Just stay there.
Don't worry about anything else. Not a lot of the young ones came back this year. Most who left looked for ways to move to the big cities. And now we rely on the Inquisition's handouts to get by.

You should think about what I said and leave this place. The folks here are the families of the workmen who gathered here to maintain the lighthouse in the past, but times have changed. This place will be abandoned sooner or later.
Jordi?
I'm listening, Uncle.
It's just... where can an Ægir go?
Amaia told me that there are places outside, in other parts of Iberia, where they aren't so harsh on the Ægir.
But I'll need to find a home for myself eventually. I need resources, connections. I... can't make a decision that easily.
I... I'm not ready yet.
...If you say so.
Right, Amaia gave me another book, one she translated herself. Not like I'd understand it anyway, though. Take it with you to kill time over in the chapel.
Okay.
Jordi walks down the street quietly.
Seeing the old man in front of him hunching over more and more, he can't help but think back to how he looked just last year.
As if to prove how time slowly overtakes us all, senility is always silent. From the moment you wake up to when you fall asleep, every second is like a gasp for air.
But Jordi never complained. He's used to this life, after all.
Uncle...
Hmm?
I'm pretty happy with my life right now. I get to help out at the chapel. Sometimes I help take care of the children, and sometimes I help patch up the injured workmen.
I even got to know that Liberi who came from out of town the other day. He's so different from everyone else. He said his name is Elysium. You don't have to worry about me.
It's been a long time since the Inquisition came to town. We don't always have to be so nervous.
That's not a chapel, lad. It's a sentry post. The Inquisition set it up to keep their eyes on us, back when we were still trying to repair the lighthouse.
But now that the coastline is peaceful and the work on the Eye of Iberia is shelved, of course the Inquisition doesn't give a toss about us anymore.
Still, to be fair, the Inquisition isn't completely useless. Only they can stop those...
Those...?
Nothing, lad. Just a few rumors going around among the workmen.
Rumors, hm...
Lad?
H-Here...?
What exactly is that...? Should I let Uncle Thiago know...?
Something bothering you, lad?
No, it's nothing.
Uncle... Just what is there out at sea?
Miss Alty! Your 'Refactored Possession' was positively spectacular!
Really? Color me surprised. I thought that silly little idea I came up with in the jungles of Sargon was a bit too avant-garde.
But turns out that doctor had someone like you under her wing? I thought you'd all be as stuffy as her.
Haha... So, where are the others?
Dan's the excitable type, so she ran straight to the beach. Aya and Frost went back to the hotel. Pretty run-down place, but kind of interesting.
Interesting? Really?
So did Kal'tsit tell you anything about us?
I only caught a word or two. Seems like you folks have something important on your hands?
I was blown away when I heard that the world-famous AUS had personal ties with Dr. Kal'tsit...
To be honest, I could say the same.
But... Still, I'm glad to have fans even out here. Gives this trip some much-needed spice.
Finally getting to meet you makes that whole excruciating month all worth it, though. Ah, if it's not too much trouble, could you please sign this poster too?
...Why'd you even bother coming back to Iberia?
That's... rather complicated. And something I sure didn't expect Alty from Alive Until Sunset, of all people, to be asking me.
You like the name of our band?
Like? Nay, I absolutely adore it.
Appreciate it.
So where is she? I figured she'd be waiting here with that know-it-all look on her face, and then end up telling us nothing in a lot of words.
Still waiting for Dr. Kal'tsit to contact me. She shouldn't have arrived much later than you all.
Hope she hurries up then... High tide is about to roll in.
...Outsiders. All outsiders. What happened all of a sudden? Did the Inquisition really loosen up its grip that much?
Yes, outsiders, all outsiders. They are very special. They carry the scent of the ocean.
Ægirs?
Possibly, possibly not. Our brothers and sisters are all afraid.
We must make haste. Scarcely has the Inquisition been so negligent... It's too suspicious. Even if the laxity of these waters is a trap, we must continue our relentless pursuit.
For the sake of our brothers and sisters, for the sake of restoring the Herald's connection to this land, we must first secure the Eye of Iberia... or destroy it!
I do not wish to indiscriminately slaughter the innocent. You were all my family, once.
Go, and have more people join us. We now have the option of choosing a profoundly vast road, greater than what Iberia promised us. Greater than this entire land.
Those people... Are the outsiders why our brothers have become restless?
It's highly likely, but I can't say for sure. We are all weak and frail. Our brothers are weak and frail. We cannot act rashly, or the Inquisition will exterminate us.
We must be... careful. Or there will be too much blood spilled on this soil, and she does not like blood. Neither us nor our brothers and sisters need die in vain.
...! Who's there?!
I knew it. Cultists, of the Church of the Deep... I step out of Iberia for just a few years, and now their scent is everywhere.
Hmm... But I'm actually starting to look forward to seeing her for once. Is she still not here?
Yeah. Dr. Kal'tsit mentioned that the Inquisition will probably keep a close eye on her, so...
That's weird. I'm sure she was there for the start of Iberia's Golden Age. She should at least be able to get an audience with their leaders. Why take such a lame approach?
Haha, very funny. Iberia's ascent was centuries ago. Even she can't be more than—
Seems her long years of experience have taught her how best to navigate this world... Alas, I'm too young and beautiful to get that stuff.
Haha, surely you jest, Miss Alty...
So... if something really were to happen to her, would you help her?
Um... If you're willing to plead on her behalf, maybe.
Is she really that important to your, uh... Rhodes Island, was it?
...To be perfectly honest, I really don't understand why she would put herself at risk like this, just to come to Iberia.
But, I fully believe that she willingly took on this responsibility. And if even Dr. Kal'tsit is prepared to risk herself for Iberia, then as an Iberian, I have even less of an excuse.
I see...
Then you'd better go take a look. Or keep waiting here. Who knows, someone might just barge in here hollering and screaming?
Hey, what's that supposed to—
Out on the plaza! I just saw one—a monster! There's a monster out there! Huh? Is anyone here?!
Wait right here, Miss Alty! I'm going to see what's going on!
...What a terrible odor.
But, Dan was right. The petals in the sea of consciousness fall into a waterfall flowing in reverse. The melody of the scales, the ballad of the rootstalks... are all ready to come forth.
Maybe I'll find a few nuggets of inspiration on this trip after all.
What... is that...?
Old Pedri was right! My grandfather wrote about these in his diary! It's a Sea Monster!
I-Is it... Is it dead? It won't start moving, right?
Sorry, excuse me, coming through—
What little light remains filters through the clouds, partially cloven by the monolithic statue. The light's blood splashes across the ground, forming shadows.
A dying Sea Terror rests quietly in the monolith's shadow, like an old man leaning against a tree to find his final respite.
Is it... already dead?
Old Pedri wasn't lying! It really is a monster! A monster from the sea appeared in Gran Faro!
I know what it's called! We've seen them before!
(So this is a Sea Terror?)
(But the coastline is so calm... And how could the Inquisition miss something this blatant?)
Its vacant, lifeless eyes seem to still be spinning, the land a vast, waterless ocean before them.
But just as Elysium mistakes it for clinging to life, its gaze once again seems to fixate on a deep, profound death, and its motions halt completely.
(It's... staring at me? Or, at something else?)
Who's going to deal with the corpse?
Just look it! How disgusting! Would it eat people if it were still alive?
The Sea Terror cannot speak, but its deformed husk shines brightly.
A large group of people gather around it. Afraid. Suspicious. But nevertheless drawn to it, as if it were lit fireworks.
Fireworks, indeed. Its death is just like fireworks. No gashes, no signs of wounds from a handcannon.
Death did not choose it. Rather, it chose death.
...No, now I'm just rambling to myself.
If this is one of those Sea Terrors from the report, then the Church of the Deep must already be closer than we thought.
Miss Amaia! Hurry, come take a look at this thing—
Now now, calm down...
Could this really be... from the sea?
I can't hide the truth from you all. Indeed... this is a monster from the sea.
Th-Then, this has to be reported to the Inquisition right away! We need to call the Penal Battalion over to deal with these monsters— No, have the Inquisitors themselves come!
Otherwise, we're all doomed!
It seems everyone is running towards the chapel?
What's going on? Hey, someone tell me what happened!
Someone found the corpse of a monster out in the plaza!
A monster...? The things Old Pedri was talking about?
That's right, everyone's clamoring to pick a Messenger to go fetch the Inquisition.
Do you know what it looks like? I heard it had fangs a meter long, and eight pairs of eyes— Hey, where are you going, Thiago?!
Head back and wait for me, Jordi. Don't set foot outside, no matter what!
But—
Okay, got it...
We'd better not get mixed up in all this chaos. Who knows what kind of vile diseases these monsters might be carrying.
A monster...
What in the world is going on?
Who's there?!
Wait!
Damn you, Ægir! Accursed Ægir! He actually went and slaughtered one of our kin!
Ugh, my wound...
You're...
Jordi?
You know me? Who are you?
I... You don't need to know, nor do you know me. But I know you, if only because you are an Ægir.
Y-You're hurt!
Yes, and will you bandage me up? Aren't you the chapel's caregiver?
I can help you, but you have to tell me how this happened.
...Very well.
Take a seat.
Just bear with it.
Nngh...
Stop moving! Luckily, you don't have any broken bones or ruptured organs... Is this a laceration wound? There's even sawdust stuck beneath the skin. How did this happen?
An Ægir killed my kin. I was involved, but managed to escape.
M-Murder?! In a small town like this? You'll have to bring this up with Uncle Thiago right away—
Hah... Haha. Mayor Thiago can't control the Ægir. How ironic. Only the Inquisition can punish him now.
B-But, he killed someone in your family.
We are always being hunted. Out of ignorance, fear, anger... the latter lingering from that disaster. Thank you, I already feel much better.
Wait, are you... Mr. Juan?
So you still remember my face, Jordi. Have you ever wanted... to gaze into the sea?
Gaze... into the sea? Uncle Thiago has taken me to the coast before, but...

lines.st: The sea was dark. The wind tugged at his hair, and the waves roared.
The meaning of a 'hometown' varies from person to person. It might be the aroma of wheat, the angle of the shadow cast across an alley, or the sound of wheels rolling over mud and snow.
But at that moment, Jordi knew that his life in Gran Faro had already been paid for in advance, with his hometown.

lines.x: The land has already forgotten the ocean's true form, but sooner or later we shall all return to the sea.
(Broken Ægirian) Thank you, Jordi. Thank you for your kindness. May infinite altruism fill the void of existence one day.
(Broken Ægirian) If you are still Ægir, Jordi, then leave us be. Turn back and return to your life. It is better that way.
(Broken Ægirian) Perhaps you too, like your parents before you, will go in search of that lighthouse.
Wh-What did you say? I didn't catch that!
(Broken Ægirian) My kindred refugees have come for me. But do not worry. Gran Faro has always been a stepping stone. Our destination is the deep sea.
(Soft slithering sound)
Jordi, Jordi. Ægir.
We shall meet again.
The sea is black.
I was four or five at the time. Old Thiago, the mayor of Gran Faro, took an armed company of adults down to the coast.

I still remember what the weather was like back then. It was hazy and turbulent, and colder than most evenings despite still being afternoon. Its coldness was not a temperature, but rather a color. One that had lost its warmth.
I found out only later that that was the last time Gran Faro did maintenance work on the Eye of Iberia. Or rather, tried to.

The Penal Battalion quickly brought back news. There was dejection in the adults' faces, but I didn't fully understand what had happened. I simply stared at the junction between sea and sky in a daze.
The sea is black.
It perfectly suits this land devoid of hope.
Mayor Thiago is here!
So what old Pedri said was true, Amaia.
Even if I wished to deny it, I'd be quickly silenced by the reality in front of our eyes.
But, what kind of person could slay a monster, then off-handedly toss it here?
I hope you'll let me handle this.
That...
If we don't dispose of it properly, I'm afraid it could become a danger down the road. Besides, is this not a rare opportunity to study a Sea Terror?
...Very well. But I'll be coming with you.
None of us know exactly what this thing is just yet, and we certainly can't rule out the possibility that its blood might attract more of its kind.
No objections here.
With me, lads. Move aside, so I can wrap up this body—
...Well, it's not as heavy as it looks. So where to, Amaia?
Follow me.
Don't just stand there gaping. Hurry on home now, people.
Are we really not going to report this to the Inquisition? What do we do if more monsters show up?
There's no need to get them involved.
But—
I said, we don't need 'em!
Ugh...
Thiago.
This town doesn't need the Penal Battalion interfering just yet. We'll handle things our own way.
If the Inquisition sent its people here and saw this thing, you'd never see another peaceful day in Gran Faro. Do you really not understand that?
But— Never mind, forget it.
Let's go, Amaia.
We're really just going to listen to him?
Old Thiago's hatred for the Inquisition goes back more than a day or two, but if something really were to happen without the Penal Battalion here, who's gonna fight the monsters? Us?
Plus, there've been more and more outsiders lately. It's like Thiago doesn't even see them. If they were to blend in with the outsiders...
I knew it, we really should send someone to the Inquisition! How are we supposed to explain all this if someone from the Penal Battalion does show up? I don't want to get branded a heretic!
Miss Amaia... and what's with that author over there...?
Let's touch base with the other members of AUS first. I feel like we can't just ignore all this.
Is the chapel normally this deserted? What happened to your God and your faith?
Not every Iberian is a practicing Lateran. Miss Alty, the town has become quite dangerous, and I need to ensure your safety until Dr. Kal'tsit arrives.
Might as well put on a show here then. Some music would help calm everyone's nerves.
Haha, that's not what I meant.
Then calm down. Let me take a stab at what I think happened.
Uhh...
Hmm... I saw... some kind of sea creature. Right, you guys called it a Sea Terror. Whether the Iberians or Kal'tsit were first to call it that doesn't really matter to me.
A Sea Terror beneath a cruelly dry sky, its observant and analytical scales with nowhere to belong. It knows very well that this is not its home. It lives, back home.
I've got it! That Sea Terror was probably hung high up on that sculpture, and it was singing the latest wave of rock-'n'-roll. Right?
...It wasn't hung up there. Nor was it singing. But you got like eighty or ninety percent of it down.
You've surprised me again, Miss Alty. Was that your Arts?
Who's to say that death itself isn't just another kind of song? That's what it taught me. And it's a real travesty of nature that the beauty of it can't be appreciated across species.
What do you think?
Fortunately, such an artform is not required by all organisms.
D-Dr. Kal'tsit! How come I couldn't get a hold of you this whole time...?
Long story short, I'm under the surveillance of the Iberian Inquisition in exchange for their trust.
We don't have much time, but the task before us is grave. Operator Elysium, go keep an eye on the two who took the Sea Terror's body away. I need to speak with Alty alone.
...Understood.
Here's a communicator. You should already be familiar with my Originium Arts, so you can also, uh, pass it on to your colleagues.
Will do.
I know you prefer to keep handovers quick and efficient, but let me say this: I'm so relieved that you're safe and sound.
If I discover anything, I'll report back right away. Heading out.
Mm. Appreciate it.
Long time no see, Doctor.
Miss Alty.
Sorry for making you come all this way. I understand this involves putting yourselves at risk to some extent.
So why are you under surveillance? You couldn't just cut a deal with them?
Time is short, and Iberia's paranoia masquerading as piety prevents me from meeting with the Inquisition's upper echelons.
Hmm... Are you in a hurry?
The other countries still do not understand what we are dealing with. They are unable to comprehend how a vast, mighty land in its Golden Age disappeared into the Silence.
And I... may as well have witnessed it with my own eyes.
Fair enough.
Where are the others?
We rarely get to go on a tour, so let them enjoy some free time. What you want is right here.
I came to you for the same reason as last time.
That said, I have little love for the sea these days. Especially after hearing what you had to say about it.
Hopefully you've had enough time to make up your mind.
Before we get to that, Doctor... Doesn't it seem like you've been working on... let's call it your bedside manner?
Like, uh, an X-ray machine with a voice greeting?
Whoops. Put my foot in my mouth again. You should already be used to that though, right?
But how quaint indeed. I didn't think anything could possibly change the way you dealt with people. Or should I say, your 'personality.'
There's always something.
Alright. Maybe I'd rather not pry into your personal affairs...
...He's here. He's still here, and it's not just him. There's more. The scent of the sea. Since when were there so many Ægir in this little town?
The Inquisition planned it.
Maybe. Maybe even now, at this very moment, there are still Inquisitors, their heads addled with their ignorant laws, intent on continuing their self-righteous deeds.
When did you have the time to wrap yourself up after escaping from the enemy?
...An Ægir helped me.
A friend?
No, just... an Ægir who loves the sea.
What do we do about our brother's body?
It's currently in the hands of Amaia and Thiago. We'll have to figure something out.
We ought to prepare a funeral for it.
A funeral is a human ritual, not ours.
Amaia has said that we should live the way our hearts dictate. Pure in desire, pure in worship, just as those ignorant Inquisitors treat their own faith.
Would that not make us equally ignorant?
Ignorance is a part of our very nature we have yet to throw away. We have not yet become them.
Sophistry.
That, I can't deny. But do you wish to see the land-dwellers torment the corpse of our brother?
Long have we lived on land, with no Herald to guide us. All we can do is seek out our own way of embracing the ocean.
But before that... we should give our brother a proper burial. We ought to mourn any senseless death.
Who would be able to slay this creature with such ease? Thiago?
What?
I'd understand if it had been surrounded by four or five Penal Battalion soldiers, or if it took the Flames of Castigation from an Inquisitor's lantern.
But... this poor Sea Terror has but a single wound on its body.
While I've never personally seen the rumored High Inquisitors fight, I'd imagine that if anyone in all of Iberia could pull this off, it'd be them.
Impossible! Nobody has seen an Inquisitor come to Gran Faro!
But our little town, which rarely sees any outsiders, has been welcoming visitor after visitor as of late. Surely this can't all be due to the Inquisition's negligence, right?
That...

Perhaps the marching of the Penal Battalion cannot be hidden from our ears, but what of the Inquisitors? If they changed out of their uniforms and concealed their lanterns and swords, how would we ever know?
You mean to say that an Inquisitor might already be here? Hidden among the outsiders?
I'm saying it's a possibility.
...I'm going on a little walk.
Thiago!
I'm sure even you understand that most people would rather have the Inquisitors help them get rid of the Sea Terrors.
I know. Of course I know. I wouldn't hate a beast for no reason. But I WILL hate those people. Those... Inquisitors.
*Sigh*...
Amaia gently caresses the Sea Terror's corpse. The fate left to it by death was not such a terrible one.
By human standards, it certainly appeared deformed and terrifying. But what about from other perspectives? How would the court of nature, of life itself, judge this perfect creature?
Alone in the empty pub, Amaia decides to stop pondering this question. Or at least, the pub appears empty. She and Thiago believe it is.
But the shadows begin to writhe, and something steps out from within, its simple outline etched out beneath the dim light's glow.
It is a person. An Ægir.
...Was this your doing?
(Ægirian) It has nothing to do with you.
You don't look like you're from the Penal Battalion.
(Ægirian) You cannot deceive me.
Wait, you're an Ægir? That makes you even less likely to be an Inquisitor.
(Ægirian) Silence is the most terrifying sound in the sea. We cannot tolerate it a second time. None of us can.
I'm not sure what you're talking about, but I think you should leave—
(Ægirian) The Bishop of Sal Viento is dead.
(Ægirian) I am not as patient as you might believe.
(Ægirian) I never thought I'd see a living Abyssal Hunter with my own eyes.
Inquisitors? ...No, impossible. How could an Inquisitor be here?
Mr. Thiago! Just now, I—
Mr. Thiago?
Get out of this place as soon as possible. You, and the rest of your lot.
You're a nice lad, I can see that. Jordi can too. But...
There's something sleeping beneath our little town, and there's nothing normal about any of this—No, Iberia's never been normal, not since then.
...I hear you. I'll leave as soon as I can.
But I think I saw something in front of the chapel just now—
Tell me, lad. Are you from the Inquisition? Are you trying to get close to Jordi so you can take him away?
Mr. Thiago?
Go ahead and lie to me. I'll fight you to the death too, outsider.
I'm not. You know as well as I do, if I were really after the Ægir, why would I wait so long?
It's just a single Sea Terror. We're by the coast, and we've seen things like this before, decades ago.
There's nothing to get worked up over, outsider. It's none of your business.
Mayor Thiago, I'm willing to help you iron out some of the issues in town.
You? There's nothing for you to do.
Even if it means Jordi's life will be in danger?
Nobody's here? Why aren't Amaia and Thiago here...?
It's his scent!
That damn Ægir, he took Amaia away?! How dare he—
—No, that's not quite right. Amaia doesn't know how to fight. He wouldn't do anything to her.
At least our brother's remains are still here.
What do we do now?
Take it away. This rotting shack is not suitable to be its coffin.
After that... We will find it a proper home. Look at it—death is nothing more than a gentle attendant. That is something that Ægir butcher could never understand.
Let's go.
Hmm... So in other words, we're what? Insurance?
To my understanding, none of you have any desire to return to the ocean and fight those monsters.
You were born of this sea, and are very powerful. But even if you regain your original forms, you will never be able to rid yourselves of those creatures.
Oh? It's that dangerous?
More than you can imagine.
To be honest though, we've never cared too much about that. We just do what we want. Most people inhabiting this great land probably think the same thing, right?
If the Seaborn can understand language and music, then I'm sure you'll find an audience, even if disaster were to befall Terra.
In fact, they're more complete creatures than humans, and have greater resistance to all sorts of things. Give them a few hundred years and maybe they'll even adapt to Originium.
I'm aware.
Mm... So answer me this, Doctor.
Us, the Ægir, Sea Terrors, the Seaborn—and all our pitiable kin lurking in the depths of the ocean—
—How are any of us different in your eyes?
You were born much later. There are many things you do not yet understand.
When Frost awoke from a deep rock formation, her first thought was to get away from those aliens and monsters.
There's nobody to communicate with in the sea, and everyone on land eventually hides their original forms and disappears. Fortunately, music can open a crack in the loneliness.
If this situation is resolved, I'll have plenty of time to discuss the past.
I also hope that none of you actually... get involved with either Iberia's or the Ægir's plans.
Why not? I thought you were eager for us to lend a helping hand.
It's been too long since I've been in touch with the Ægir. The tides are crying out, and a lack of information hampers my predictions.
We do not yet know the true origins of this calamity, nor are we able to infer any possibilities.
Worst-case scenario, even you won't have any way to— *sigh*... It seems we have uninvited guests.
...Huh?
They already have the nerve to show themselves openly in town?
Someone's knocking at the door.
Without a word, Kal'tsit turns her eyes to the chapel's doors. The thick wooden slabs had endured an unfathomable amount of turmoil over the years, each mark upon them a chronicle of the past.
She knows there is something outside them, and what it is. What she cannot imagine, however, is that the Church of the Deep's followers would already dare to be so brazen.
Or perhaps...
Outsiders. A grotesque scent, belonging to those strange Ægir.
The two cultists step into the chapel. This Lateran edifice, once wrapped in the cloth of faith, now drapes these two faceless figures.
In their arms, they hold their 'brother,' a Sea Terror—or perhaps just a being stripped of all other names, now nothing more than an expired lifeform.
Look what they're holding in their arms...
A Sea Terror. They don't intend to fight.
The cultist carrying his brother's body passes by Kal'tsit without turning his head, as if he does not even see the two standing in his path.
It is as if he has come all the way from the doors of the chapel to step into the depths of martyrdom.
(Broken Ægirian) My brother, o brother. Hear me out.
(Broken Ægirian) It is our incompetence that led to your untimely fate. It is our cowardice that prevented you from returning to the sea.
(Broken Ægirian) Forgive me, my brother. I can yet hear your voice.
(Broken Ægirian) The people of this world all believe death to be the terminus of all life, but we understand that once our individual forms are transcended, even death itself is powerless.
He lays his brother's remains upon the ground and draws a deep breath. He falls to his knees, his eyes filled with sorrow.
(Broken Ægirian) Until we return to the ocean, I shall be your coffin. I will take you back to the deeps.
(Broken Ægirian) Come, my brother. May you slumber eternally. We shall become one.
The oral ingestion of protein is an act of feeding, yet he did not intend to consume his brother.
He was simply forced to use a more primordial method of bringing the two together as one.
'Chimera.'
Urgh... Aaahh—
What is he doing?
—Assimilation. The simplest method of forcefully penetrating his own torso with the Sea Terror's body.
Should we stop him? It's pretty gross, and totally lacking in aesthetics.
Someone will. But not us.
Juan! What are you doing!
Haah... I... It's still alive... I can feel it... What did you say?
The Inquisition is here!
You... Urgh... Graaah...
Repulsive.
Though this chapel may be deserted, it is still a sacred place to the Iberian Inquisition. And yet, you dare openly blaspheme our laws and creeds.
Someone from the Inquisition actually snuck in? But what happened to that Ægir?!
Juan!
You...! Curse you... Hrrgh... Damn Iberian!
Kill him!
Graaah!

The Sea Terror of the grim lake crests and troughs as the mountains roiling in the twilight. Carrying the scent of rotting flesh, the traitor nearly usurps the throne of his new home, screeching gleefully.

To think that you'd target such a small, inconspicuous town after Sal Viento. Even now, the Inquisition underestimates the scope of the Church of the Deep's influence. Indeed, your taint is pervasive.
Juan! The Inquisitor! You have to—!
You're even weaker than any Church of the Deep remnants I've seen. Why do you insist on staying in this unremarkable town?
Answer me.
Jordi!
Uncle Thiago? What's going on?
Quick, pack up. We have to go.
Wait, what? Where are we going?
The Inquisition is coming.
...What?
The cultists, the monsters from the sea...
Are we leaving this town for good?
We?
No, no, just you. When the Inquisition finds out there are cultists here, the Ægir will be their first target.
I don't care what happens to the cultists, or even the rest of us... We've lived here too long. We don't know how to live anywhere else.
But you're different. You've always wanted to see the world outside. You're young and talented.
But, I... this is all so sudden.
It's an opportunity for change, lad. Your last chance. Be brave.
Even if you somehow survive the Inquisition's interrogation, all that's waiting for you here is a life spent in fear of the ocean and the Sea Terrors.
If you leave, though, your fate will finally be in your own hands. Not stuck in that little chapel.
That place is too small for you. You shouldn't just—
But, I... It's so sudden.
There's no time to hesitate, lad. You must be strong, like your parents and their parents before them.
Did my parents really give their lives for Iberia?
We don't have time for this. Come, we must pack.
It's been so long since we've had a traveling trader stop by... all your clothes are so old. You can buy new ones so easily out there. All the more reason to leave, don't you think?
But why would the Inquisition be looking for Ægir? Couldn't cultists just as well be Liberi or Feline?
Ægir come from the sea, same as the monsters and the disaster.
Wait... have you seen them?
No, I... I mean, Uncle, you knew? You knew they were here?
Yes... we all knew there was something to the rumors.
Juan was one of them. He went into an empty warehouse to hide from the streets. He was hurt, and... he had no hostility towards me.
Right, because you're Ægir. I know they've gone mad, that they're not normal.
But why? ...Are you sheltering them?
None of that matters anymore. You'll be leaving soon.
No, I don't want to.
What?
I... I was born here in Gran Faro. If I run away just to hide from the Inquisition, I... I don't know.
You what? Do you have any idea what you're saying?
No Ægir has ever made it back in one piece after the Inquisition gets them! If you're LUCKY, you might return missing a few body parts, or maybe your sanity.
Most simply disappear though. Some say they've been imprisoned, others that they've been executed. No one comes back! No one!
The Inquisition will burn everything to the ground in their 'mercy,' before the monsters and cultists even get a chance. Nothing cleanses like fire!
U-Uncle Thiago, calm down!
The angry old man sees in Jordi the shadow of himself, arriving in this land all those years ago, hoping to construct a new garden out of the ruins.
Decades have passed, and those who were once young are no longer so. Yet what have they built in Gran Faro? What has become of Iberia?
Their efforts have done nothing to change the tidings of fate, and the home that they put so much of their passion into now stands on the precipice of collapse.
A vast helplessness engulfs Thiago, like the dark clouds over the sea.
No one...
No one... no one has ever come back. Not Marin, not anyone else. Gran Faro is already burning. Be it the Inquisition or the monsters, there's no future for us here.
Uncle Thiago...
I'll never be able to face your parents if anything happens to you, Jordi.
I... I know, I...
C-Can I go see the ocean?
Gaaaaaaaah!
Dream now, and may your sleep be full of nightmares.
Leave them to me, Kal'tsit. Soon enough, they will confess where their dark and dank schemes take root.
No doubt.
(Wow, the old man's with you, Doctor?)
You are... Alive Until Sunset. A rock band. I must admit I don't understand these new-fangled trends.
Have faith in the divinity of song then.
Iberia hasn't recorded a direct sighting of a Feranmut for centuries, much less an interaction. If your music represents your views on human society, then that would certainly pique my interest.
We haven't paid attention to that sort of thing since 'Deep Color in the Sea.' It's a topic that's all too familiar, you see.
Ha... I wonder, what music would you put to Iberia? The most surprising thing I've found at my age is how much still surprises me.
As we discussed, the Inquisition will prepare a means for you to travel to the Eye of Iberia as soon as possible, Kal'tsit.
You will eliminate any obstacles in your path.
...That Sea Terror. The one that's embedded in the guy.
There's the scent of an Ægir on it. A different scent though, one more like us. The scent of the sea.
What?
Thiago's steps are small, but quick. He seems burdened by age, yet surprisingly energetic.
It was all Jordi could do to keep up with his pace. He said he wanted to see the ocean.
He did not know why he said that. Even now, he does not know whether it is he who wants to see it, or Thiago.
'I'm an Ægir,' Jordi thinks. 'The sea is my home.'
'But how do I breathe down there? How did my ancestors live underwater?'
Questions pop into his head one after another, questions he had been curious about from a young age. But Thiago is Liberi, and cannot answer his questions.

lines.st: The Ægir came from the ocean. Why would they drown in the ocean?

lines.x: The sea...
When was the last time I saw it?
If the Penal Battalion were still here, they would've taken us in for questioning by now.
S-Sorry for asking to do this.
...It's fine.
Look at the coastline. Barren and decrepit. Decades ago, we had big dreams of building an impregnable fortress here.
Today, there's a Penal Battalion outpost several clicks to the northeast, and another on the cliffs to the west.
Everything else? A lie... or perhaps a dream in the true sense. Reality is before our eyes, reminding us that the old Iberia will never return.
Thiago whistles. Jordi rarely sees him do so.
A change seems to have come over the normally gloomy mayor, turning him into a young worker once again, passionate about building his home.
Tsk, it's all in the past. We failed. Gran Faro is the final resting place of our failures.
You mentioned your parents, didn't you lad?
I did...
I don't remember much of the ocean. Everyone talks about Ægir and the Islanders, but none of it feels real. I was born on land.
Aye. Your parents, or more accurately your grandparents, came from a place called Ægir, a great country deep beneath the ocean.
That's where you were from, lad.
Ægir.
Years ago, the dream we had for Gran Faro wasn't the lifeless town you see today. It would have been a fortress, one that laughs at any siege.
We would have built defenses on the coast around it, protected our country from calamity, heralded her revival, and rebuilt our home. All with our own hands.
We really thought so.
Jordi does not interrupt. Thiago grows increasingly excited, a light returning to his dull and cloudy eyes.
Every hair on his body stands on end. He changes his posture. The sea breeze brushes against his brow, and he even looks grander somehow.
The last step of our grand design, spanning half a century, was to return to the Eye of Iberia and relight the lighthouse.
The lighthouse?
A relic of the Golden Age, spared from destruction by the Profound Silence.
Not even calamity could break us. We marched ever forward, our hearts filled with hope.
Marin wore that blue hat of hers. She said we'd become heroes, that our names would live on.
Your grandparents, your parents... two generations of Gran Faro gave everything they had for this blueprint. Their greatness should have been immortalized forever.
But...
But now there's nothing left.
You see that ship? There used to be many ships at the port. The Penal Battalion should have taken it, but they've forgotten all about it.
All that's left is some junk, a town out in the middle of nowhere, more troubles than I can count, people stuck in a dead end, and—
And that ship.
Yes. That ship.
My parents, and the grandparents I've never met... were they proud of their duty?
This isn't the first time you've asked that.
You asked it when you first learned to talk, and when you started working at the chapel. You asked it many times, pointing at the dust-covered notebooks and schematics at home.
I... I remember.
Yes, lad.
No matter how many times you ask, my answer is always 'yes.' No matter the outcome, they never lost hope after that great disaster, and gave their all to the cause.
Your parents even volunteered for our final attempt to reach the Eye.
Don't blame them for abandoning you. They were carrying what was left of all our dreams. So many years had come and gone. We HAD to succeed.
The last we saw of them was when their fleet set sail from right here.
They were heroes. Marin said we would all become heroes, but now none of us are, except for the dead.
I see...
So my father and mother...
But you know what laid waste to it all? In the midst of despair, we managed to muster the hope to relight the lighthouse. And what came of it?
The Inquisition. They arrested the Ægir. They said there were Church of the Deep spies among us.
We were fighting for Iberia, but the Inquisition took everything from us—our pride, our dignity, our dreams... everything.
Do you understand now?
Leave this hopeless town, leave Iberia and go as far as you can. Go to another country, go to a better place.
There's nothing left here, in Gran Faro.
The sea!
This is the wrong way. Are we just gonna leave our instruments in that damp room?
No, I can smell the waves. The sea is just ahead.
What do waves smell like?
Like cotton.
Using olfactory vocabulary to express visual elements...
But you're right. The sea's right up ahead.
You...
An Ægir, and of a different breed from the ones who grew up on land. You must be that strange sort of Ægir.
What did they call you again? Oh right, Abyssal Hunters.
Gladiia frowns. She rarely expresses her emotions in such complex ways.
It came from her instincts as an Abyssal Hunter, as a Consul, as an Ægir technician, as a soldier.
You are... AUS.
You know them.
Yes, by chance.
Why so mysterious? Well, whatever, I'm just glad our name is known even among the Ægir.
Alty once visited that ship, following your scent trail. But this is the first time we've met in person.
I'm Aya, main vocalist of AUS. Dan here is our drummer.
Greetings, guests from the sea.
You're of the sea too. A pleasure to meet you.
When I came to the land, I sought Kal'tsit to learn more about you—about your terrestrial kin.
Oh... if you're talking about the things on land, then I guess we're kin, technically. Not sure I can say the same about the ones in the sea.
What are you here for? The doctor?
Just an appointment.
We seek a way back to Ægir.
Back to Ægir, huh... The three of you, together?
We are not so easily separated.
Aw, what a great friendship. What about the one in the back? Is she shy? Why doesn't she say anything?
Something doesn't seem right with her. Her brain is tormented. Is she sick?
You could say so.
Poor thing.
—Mm!
What is it, Dan?
I just felt something up my spine. Like an electric shock to my brain, then a strange—
—Voice.
Unusually quiet, Specter gazes at the sky.
She closes her eyes. Waves brush against chaotic thoughts. This place is far from the coast, but something is touching her consciousness.
It is in the wind, among the clouds, through the land, from the sea.
A voice. I can hear it.
Hmm... I don't hear anything.
The music of the sea. The children of the sea are crying.
It is... a song.
The scent of Sea Terrors. Lots of them. The town is under attack.
H-Hey! How did you get in here?
A-Are you an Inquisitor?! The ones you're dragging are—
Go home.
These two are under suspicion of colluding with heretics. I am performing my duty as an Iberian Inquisitor.
Leave now, unless you want to be caught up in battle. Their blood will draw enemies.
A-An Inquisitor?! When did the Inquisition arrive? W-Were those outsiders your informants?
I, I'm not a cultist! I don't have anything to do with those guys!
Do you know these two?
Them? Juan...? It's Juan? I always thought he looked suspicious. He—
That's enough.
No need to torment yourself. Go home, and be sure your doors and windows are locked.
A-Alright...
You can come out now.

lines.n: Church of the Deep Cultist A
lines.x: An Inquisitor... no, a High Inquisitor!

lines.n: Church of the Deep Cultist B
lines.x: What's the Inquisition doing here?

lines.n: Church of the Deep Cultist A
lines.x: Amaia and Thiago have gone missing. Do the Ægir have something to do with it?

lines.n: Church of the Deep Cultist B
lines.x: You killed Juan, you bastard!
Not quite, he yet lives. I intend to extract some answers from him.
You are different from the enemies I know. Your minds are your own, and you are working under clear directions.
But if you're truly sane and awake, then you should understand—
—That you stand no chance against me, nor any other brave soldier of the Inquisition.

lines.n: Church of the Deep Cultist A
lines.x: Tch...
Why give up your lives? What meaning is there in your futile sacrifice?
What have the accursed tides promised you? What are you scheming on this barren shore?
I have many questions, enemies of Iberia.
And I can only hope your god has answers.
You're not gonna help?
You aren't rising from your seat either.
I'm not needed.
Strictly speaking, I'm still a prisoner of Iberia. It is not my place to worry for a High Inquisitor.
Let's talk about the two of us.
We'll stay in this town on the coastline, for a while.
We know how to follow stage directions. If you and your Abyssal Hunters fail, we'll take the Iberians and pull back to an appropriate position.
Assuming the old man outside listens to us, anyway.
...You're willing to help the people on the land.
Frost is young, I'm about the same age as Dan, and Aya maybe a little older.
There's little left for us to love, except... music.
Frailty of body and mind has driven humans to seek breakthroughs within their short lifespans.
And what do you know, they pulled it off.
...Indeed.
But the sea's not a fan of heavy metal.
We don't have a choice. As far as survival is concerned, the land is the lesser evil.
It would not be difficult to get this country to listen. Reveal a few secrets, hint at old wounds, and promise that the same disaster will not strike again.
Can it really be that easy?
Even simpler, if promises can be considered lies.
Monsters! Monsters!
Where's the Inquisition?! Anyone?! Help!
Grr... grr...
Eek!
As ever, Kal'tsit picks the worst meeting locations.
What's going on here?
T-Thank you... A-Are you an Ægir too? From the outside?
Ah, you refer to AUS. Ægir, eh...
A-Are you here to fight them? You took out those monsters in the blink of an eye...
Your attitude towards the Ægir seems different in this town.
There used to be a lot of them around here, until about twenty years ago.
Did the Inquisition send you? Sh-Should we ask for their help?
None of my concern.
But unless you intend to get hurt, I recommended hiding until this is over.
A-Alright.
This place, they're everywhere.
Not as bad as Sal Viento, but it's still strange.
This town has not been consumed by rot. There are even those leading more or less normal lives here.
The waves have not touched this place.
Then the flotsam has gone into hiding.
No surprise there. If our target is that ship, then Kal'tsit must have chosen this place for a reason.
Should we split up?
I'll find Kal'tsit. You and Shark handle the Sea Terrors.
Right.
They are weak, but don't get hurt. Weakness is often bait.
...Shark?
Hm?
Take care of her, Skadi. I still have trouble getting used to how she is now.
I know.
Monsters?! Where did they come from?
This way!
Oh, er, right!
They're relatively few in number, but surprisingly coordinated. Is someone leading them?
And how could they have just popped up out of nowhere? Where are they hiding?
Is it... this way?
(Getting closer... there's someone here!)
One falls, another takes their place. We give our lives only to buy time.
No one here can stand against a High Inquisitor. All who resist are doomed.
In death lies hope. Do not squander it.
Yes... we must hasten our departure. But it is a shame to abandon the closest outpost we have to the Eye of Iberia...
We need but one to reach the Eye... Who goes there?!
(Crap!)
Liberi! Don't let him get away!
Tch.
Gran Faro will be no more. Is this really what you want?
Our interests are aligned. This shouldn't have to happen.
You're the only one who can stop the both the Inquisition and your own people.
I'll kill you, Amaia. Soon.
My, my... I thought we were on a first-name basis, Ulpianus.
Perhaps you intended to make your move once we'd arrived at the lighthouse? But you know full well that's not our destination.
We're going someplace further, and it's there that we'll make our choice.
Aren't the Abyssal Hunters all of one mind? Why haven't you gone back to your squad?
What are you thinking?
You never saw what I saw.
Ah, yes... the oldest story in the book. They never saw what you saw, either.
Did you lose trust in each other?
Ægir is united by the bonds of blood.
I chose to go into exile in order to achieve our common goal.
Uh huh...
Stay away from them, Amaia.
Even on dry land, Gladiia, Skadi and Laurentina can wipe this town from existence with little more than a shrug.
Hmph.
The hand that Amaia has placed on the book moves ever so slightly.
Unperturbed, she smiles back at the provocation.
You're right. Thank you for your concern.
I need that ship.
I must reach it before Gladiia. They cannot return to Ægir knowing nothing of what's going on.
Finally shook them off!
How can they be hiding this many people? Is this town some kind of clown car?
There's nothing but barrens to the north.
So where are they coming from?
Hm.
A pleasure to meet you, ma'am.
You must be the Abyssal Hunter that Kal'tsit mentioned.
Is this your work?
Iberia is not always so messy. I beg your pardon.
Gladiia is quiet for a moment.
In her long years on dry land, she has seen how weak and backwards the terrestrial states are, but also the strength of the Inquisitors at Sal Viento.
Kal'tsit's words echo in her mind as she thinks on how to live alongside those above water.
The silence lasts only a moment.
I am Gladiia, Technology Consul of Ægir, and the Abyssal Hunters' Chief Designer of Warfare.
For the first time, she speaks to a land-dweller as a representative of Ægir.
Her counterpart nods, sheathes his rapier, and strides over the silent carcass of the Sea Terror.
Carmen, High Inquisitor of the Iberian Inquisition. I see the legends of Ægir hold true.
You're different from the others.
I have a few more years under my belt.
Where's Kal'tsit?
She's speaking to another guest from the ocean in the chapel.
Gladiia turns to walk away when—
If you've come to speak to the doctor, I'm sure she's shared the same information with you.
...Perhaps.
What do you think of her suggestion?
We can talk after I've confirmed she's unharmed.
We have no reason to harm someone who wishes to protect Iberia from the bottom of her heart.
So you say.
I've seen more foolishness in my brief time on land than I have for half a lifetime.
If it's been such a brief time, how can you be so sure?
The worst of times brings out the worst in people. Iberia's current reality is far from gilded.
Regardless, I'm not here to debate.
If she's in the chapel behind you, then—
You'll never reach the Eye of Iberia without the Inquisition's help.
It was not destroyed in the Profound Silence. If the Stultifera remains afloat, then the Eye of Iberia is the only way to find it in the vastness of the ocean.
What Iberia can offer, I can achieve on my own with only slightly more effort.
I suppose I have something to look forward to then, Gladiia.
How many left?
Four or five, maybe. Some have departed to seek sanctuary elsewhere.
Very well. Burn these documents. We don't have time for them.
Gran Faro... I once called this place home.
You are still bound by such distractions?
No... no more. Burn them all.
The Inquisition must not find them.
We embrace this knowledge, and promote harmony through enlightenment. The Inquisition, in their ignorance and selfishness, calls it heresy.
Let's go. We will find a way to reestablish contact.
We too will embrace the ocean, someday.
(Are they gone? Is this one of their hideouts? And here I thought it was just a struggling restaurant. It's only two blocks from the chapel, for crying out loud.)
(They're burning their papers? I have to put the fire out!)
(The flames are too strong. I have to save what I can—)
Yowch, that's hot!
Whew... are these blueprints? Some sort of equipment...? The stamp of approval has been burned to ash.
Historical records of Iberia... theses on ocean currents...
What are they planning? Breo... gan? Royal shipwright?
And what's this? A drawing of that statue in the square?
Put that down, Liberi. Let it burn as it should.
You—
Elysium has always been confident in his ability to talk his way out of a situation.
In this case, however, he does not get a chance to, barely avoiding the touch of cold steel.
Hey, don't you know it's rude to strike without warning?!
Dr. Kal'tsit! Their goal is—
Irene.
Yes, maestro... I mean, sir!
You seem distracted.
I... I'm fine.
Gran Faro is a tiny town, so small that most maps don't even bother to list it.
But this is a request from Lord Carmen himself, and it involves Dr. Kal'tsit and those Ægir.
I know, sir. I—
If you still haven't found your answer, I will not give you permission to join the mission.
I'd already made up my mind when I first decided to follow in your footsteps and become an Inquisitor.
Yes, before you even knew the question. An answer is worthless if it cannot explain what you see and hear.
Don't die a worthless death, Irene. You know what we're fighting against. And you must find your own answer.
I—
...Yes, sir.
Lady Kal'tsit.
Gladiia's tone does not change, but her footsteps reflect her mood.
A blink of the eye, and she is by Kal'tsit's side.
Good work, Gladiia.
The Inquisition didn't do anything to you, did they?
The fact that Iberia has not yet sunk into the sea is proof that they still retain a shred of humility and vigilance.
You found a way, as promised.
Yes. However...
Even if Iberia is willing to let go of their fears, what about Ægir?
What about you?
If this temporary cooperation is necessary to help us return to the ocean... so be it.
Finally, an Ægir with some sense, right? Like the waves themselves, like the rays and texture of the sea.
Hello there, special Ægir. I believe you've already met our other members?
Yes. You're also a member of AUS?
Alty, bassist. Nice to meet you.
How are things outside?
The cultists are out in force, but individually they're rather weak.
My Hunters can take care of everything.
The Inquisition will only work with us once we've taken care of the problems in Gran Faro.
Then there's no time to waste.
Gladiia raises her lance.
At the same time, she senses that something is not right.
There, among the Sea Terrors... but how could it be...?
Kal'tsit here.
Dr. Kal'tsit! Their goal is—
...Elysium?
What's the matter?
One of our operators is in trouble.
Something lurks here.
As we discussed, Alty, the members of AUS can stay ashore.
We'll handle this as quickly as possible.
Sure, we can wait.
And we'll be happy to provide some BGM for your campaign.
...Too weak.
They keep coming. Buying time?
Or spreading... something.
I must find the leader.
—Gkh—!
Specter.
Ah... Skadi. Long time no see.
Something the matter?
...Are you alright?
I'm fine, just a little tired.
Yes... We're fighting, right? The scent of the ocean... reminds me of home.
But, home? My home... huh...
Gkh—!
The sea.
The scent of the sea grows stronger. Are we home, Skadi? Is this the way home?
...It will be, Specter. Laurentina. I promise.
Tell me then... what's that before my eyes?
What?
A figure strides down the street, humming a tune.
The streets are a mess in the aftermath of the panicking townsfolk, but it hardly bothers Frost as she plucks at the air and imagines music.
Hmph!
(Slithering sounds)
The Sea Terrors keep away from her. No, they ignore her. Is she... playing an instrument?
Does it sound nice?
I don't know.
Then ask.
Wait!
*pant*... *pant*...
(Did I shake him?)
Dammit?!
Was it when we first met? No, he didn't even touch me.
Those documents must be destroyed, Iberian.
Obey, and you may keep your limbs. Attached, if you prefer.
(Shit, he's fast...)
Weren't your friends only burning them because there wasn't time to save them all?
With your skills, you could just as easily have taken them yourself. So why the need to burn them?
'Friends?'
The man growls from the bottom of his throat, a sound born of humiliation.
We are not 'friends.'
Then what?
It doesn't concern you. Surrender the documents, and leave this place.
Whether you're with the Inquisition, or with another country...
(He stopped.)
Yes... you've always been sharp.
The mysterious Ægir gazes into the distance.
'Friends.'
(Ægirian) We may be bound by blood, but you must stay away from Ægir for now.
Elysium evades the attack, but it's immediately clear why he managed to.
The sword's aura rips the charred papers to shreds, sending the splintered truth drifting into the wind.
Crap—
(Ægirian) They cannot return to Ægir just yet. Allowing everyone to act on their own will only accelerate the destruction.
(Ægirian) Even though Ægir has already chosen the path of self-annihilation.
No more Sea Terrors approach.
Unsurprising numbers, for the coast. Now, where are you hiding?
You... *cough* No! Not the lantern!
Answer me. What do you intend for Gran Faro?
That lantern! Get it off me—
Answer me.
Ugh...
It... It's...
What are you pointing at?
Carmen looks back in the direction that the cultist is indicating.
A statue stands in the middle of the square.
Ah.
Gran Faro. The Great Lighthouse. Of course. You're seeking the lighthouse of Gran Faro.
Stop... stop it... ugh...
The nobles once called those great monuments the Eye of Iberia. Dozens of them once stood on Iberia's coastline.
Even had the Witch King's Tower in Leithanien not been toppled, it would still have been dwarfed by these guardians of the sea.
They are the single greatest mark that human civilization has ever left on this land.
The sea... *cough* the Eye of Iberia...
The miracle... destroyed the sacrilege... argh... take that lantern away!
The Profound Silence destroyed most of the lighthouses, and disabled the rest.
What are you plotting?
I see. So you knew...
You knew that one remained operational. That the goal of the Gran Faro project was to protect what was left of the Eye of Iberia.
That's why you've been lurking here, awaiting your chance.
You...
I never expected there to be traitors in the very heart of the Inquisition. Where are they?
Ha... hehehe...
While you're here... babbling away...
Look under your feet... and come face to face... with your pathetic... narrow-mindedness...
What?
(Unsettling slithering sounds)
Hm.
The Sea Terrors are... glowing?
What is this luminescence?
Get away from it!
Ah—
Mon3tr!
(Impatient growling)
Meltdown.
(Agitated roars)
Hello.
(Peaceful riff)
There is a familiar scent on you... Do you know what it is?
(Spirited riff)
A pleasant melody, but not the one that I know.
Then, how does the one you know go?
When she prayed, the stars ceased to shimmer♪
When she wept, the night let out a smile♪
When she laments, the anguish sprawled upon her madness♪
...A song of Ægir. A sad song.
But I don't like it. It belongs in the past. It lacks passion, and wallows in futile sentimentality—the same as Ægir itself.
A shame.
You've come home, Ægir.
Home? Me?
Sing.
In song, you will rediscover yourself.
Song?
What memories will singing recall?
Just sing. You will encounter your past. You must eventually find and face your destiny, or it will find you.
That's how it has ever been, since I left the ocean.
What a strange woman. But I don't mind singing.
Then sing.
La la la, la la la♪
When she prays, the stars ceased to shimmer♪
As always, there's such sorrow in your song.
Who are... you?
I never imagined we'd meet again in such circumstances, Laurentina.
Exactly as the reports say. Such a curious creature.
(Growls in warning)
Easy now, no need to get excited. Seems you understand human speech.
There are parasites living on these Sea Terrors, but I've never seen them on land before.
The Iberians call it the 'Nethersea Brand.'
An eerie glow has been occasionally seen off the coast of Iberia since the Profound Silence, the earliest known instance dating back to 17 years ago.
Our scientists believe it to be some sort of algal bloom.
So, have the Ægir also encountered these... unsettling things?
Seldom, but the Academy's garden has no shortage of luminescent ornamental plants.
So the Ægir do know what the word 'ornamental' means.
Nethersea brand. An apt name. It is the sea leaving its mark on the land.
At its heart, its spread is no different from the Sea Terrors we saw at Sal Viento.
Do you mean to say this phenomenon is but another form of their evolution?
I see the biologists of Iberia are lacking in many ways.
We've encountered some complications, but this is far from the worst-case scenario.
The Inquisition has noted an alarming increase in the variety of Sea Terrors.
