Most notably at Coastline 3, where after a mere four or five days our enemies were unrecognizable.
As I said, the ocean encroaches upon the land in its own way.
Now, there's an operator from Rhodes Island I must rescue.
I'll accompany you.
No, it'll be more expedient for you to work with the Hunters to eliminate the threat as quickly as possible.
I'd assumed Iberia would treat her with more caution.
Indeed she should be, but for now we have a clearer enemy to deal with.
What will you do, consul of Ægir? Continue to bicker over your country's pride?
Ten minutes. I'll take care of everything and meet up with my Hunters.
Then, we can talk about how to reach that lighthouse.
I'll remain here then. Law has been absent from this chapel for far too long, but thankfully someone has been polishing it.
It will be the only light in this war. The Iberian Inquisition is here, even if it is I alone.
Hey, what's going on?
Thiago! Where were you?
The Church of the Deep's cultists appeared out of nowhere! And the town's covered in monsters!
Oh... and...
And what? Why did they attack the town?
Where's Amaia? Did the monsters harm you?
They... they...
They didn't harm anyone... they're just fighting... an Inquisitor.
No one told the Inquisition! I swear!
They showed up not long after we found the carcass in the square. It might've been their work in the first place, now that I think of it.
What's going on, Uncle? Oh, good afternoon there.
You look...
Jordi, come with me.
Uncle?
Quiet.
Th-Thiago, it wasn't us... we couldn't have contacted the Inquisition in such a short time, no matter how fast we were.
I know. We need to protect Jordi and explain things.
Maybe someone went to them earlier. A week ago, a month ago, who knows? Rumors of cultists have been spreading for a while now.
I-It wasn't me! Or my husband!
Could it be Juan? Maybe the Inquisition found him?
You...
You knew Juan was a member of the Church of the Deep? When did you find out?
Jordi! Go!
Uh... alright.
Thiago!
Oh... he's gone.
Never mind him. What about the town?
I-I don't know. The Inquisitor and the outsiders are fighting the monsters. There's nothing we can do about it.
Tsk... the Inquisitor will be coming for us once he's done.
Surely not... wait, what are you thinking?
The best-case scenario would be both sides taking each other out...
What?! You can't possibly be serious!
Think about it. Have we really lost that many people to Sea Terrors? Compared to how much damage the Inquisition has done over the years.
B-But...
When they come for you, can you prove that you're not a cultist?
What are we supposed to do then?
If the Inquisitor can really kill that many Sea Terrors, the only way to avoid suspicion would be to hand over someone even more suspicious.
For example, an Ægir linked to the Church of the Deep.
Gkh...
The others have pulled out. But Amaia is nowhere to be seen.
Both an Inquisitor, and the Abyssal Hunters. I've only heard them mentioned in passing by Amaia.
Here on land, though, they're not as troublesome as the Inquisitor.
Find Amaia. Take her, and delay the Abyssal Hunters.
Go. Our siblings have fallen. We are the last ones standing. The Inquisition must not be allowed to take any more of our comrades.
(Shifts head in agreement)
Jordi, hurry!
O-Okay...
(What's happened to the town... are those Sea Terrors too?)
It's the Inquisition.
Maybe a High Inquisitor, seeing how he's come to uproot a Church hideout all by himself.
Sea Terrors and cultists have appeared in Gran Faro. There's no explaining this away.
Take this, lad, and the lamp your father left you. Keep them safe.
I—
You're not a child anymore, Jordi Fontanarossa. I've told you everything. This is your last chance to embrace a new life.
I... I always wanted to tell you the truth, convince you to leave, but I...
I know, Uncle. You're like a father to me. Gran Faro is our home. If I leave here... I won't have anything left.
It was going to happen, sooner or later. All the young folks want to leave; you're the exception.
I'll go with you. Sea Terrors are dangerous, but I know how to deal with them. This is our last chance to slip past the Inquisitors, though.
What's that sound?
...The door?
Uhh.... Huh?
You! Not even Sea Terrors eat people, but you people have no such qualms!
Say something! Pyotr! Manuel! I know it's you out there! Cowards!
What's going on?
They've blocked the door.
What? But why?
Because you're an Ægir.
They must've surrounded the place. We'll go up to the attic and leave through the roof.
Do I... know you?
Look at you, Laurentina... What happened to your nun's habit? You looked so good in it.
Ah, you found where you belonged, right? The Abyssal Hunters found you, and they want to bring you home?
Laurentina... my name is Laurentina.
The name of the vast creation's master.
What is this 'vast creation's master?'
The ocean, or its avatar.
Something's not right with your state of mind... You shouldn't be so— Oh, were you awake? When you killed Quintus, for instance?
Quin...tus?
You touched the sea, but did not immediately proceed to Ægir? I thought you people longed for home.
Or do you have somebody on land, gently guiding you?
Why did we have to reunite under such circumstances, Laurentina?
Your hands, are so cold.
There was a time when there were no secrets between us.
Now, though...
Specter!
When you remember my name, come and find me. If you still remember this conversation, that is.
If your mind is your own then, I will tell you the whole truth.
Farewell, Laurentina.
Ah...
Who was that?
I don't know... but she seems to know me?
She has no scent. She's not Seaborn, at least not like Quintus.
We don't have time for her.
Clean up the Sea Terrors, and we'll regroup with the Captain.
Hey, there aren't any Sea Terrors around here, are there?
Only dead ones. It's so quiet here... Did the Inquisition take care of them all?
All the better. Come, help me shut them inside. Somebody go fetch the Inquisitor!
A-Alright, it's blocked. But why are we doing this to Mayor Thiago when everything's such a mess right now?
Because we're all off to the gallows when things STOP being a mess, unless we do something about it. Go check out the chapel and get someone from the Inquisition.
Me? But what if there are still monsters...?
Huh? Is there someone on the roof?
Wha—?
It's Jordi and Thiago! Get them! Don't let them get away!
Run!
I, I know!
Get out of town, and don't let the Inquisitors or those bastards catch you!
Alright!
*pant* Keep running north... *pant*... There's a messenger station there that might still be in operation...
Alright!
I put some money in your bag... *pant*... get out of town, get out of the country!
A-Alright!
Jordi!
I'm listening!
Thiago's footsteps slow down.
He looks on as the young Ægir runs desperately down the street.
Run! Don't look back! Keep running!
That's right... get out of here... never look back.
Don't let them out of town!
You were right, lad.
This is our home, and no amount of waiting or complaining will change that.
(Ægirian) Farewell, my child!
Thiago! Where's the Ægir!
Coward!
W-What the hell are you doing? You'll doom us all! We'll all be branded heretics by the Inquisition, just like the old days!
Are you more afraid of the Inquisition than the Sea Terrors? More afraid of your own people than monsters?
We already lost everything to the Inquisition once. Do you want that to happen again?
Pah... your fist is like rubber...
You—wait, what's that... they're still here?!
(Ominous slithering sounds)
I, I've got to get back to the chapel. The Inquisitors will protect me—
Take that, you coward!
Are, are you mad?! Don't you see the Sea Terrors?
What about the Sea Terrors?!
Did Sea Terrors eat Gran Faro? No, it was you people... you're worse than the Profound Silence.
You've slaughtered compassion with selfishness, whitewashed your own cowardice, and blamed the disaster on the Ægir!
No, the monsters, they're getting closer—screw it, nutjob, I don't have time for this!
Of course, you coward. When they took Marin, when they took the Ægir, you were right there under the banner of the Penal Battalion!
Murderer!
Don't think the rest of us are gonna humor you just because you fell in love with an Ægir way back when!
Come at me, coward! You'll get it from me!
(Sharp sound of breathing)

lines.st: The two men become increasingly bloodied as they pummel each other, while Sea Terrors rush by, ignoring them entirely.
They look decidedly out of place.

lines.x: *pant*... *pant*...
Thiago...? Uncle Thiago?
*pant*... *pant*...
Uncle—?
*pant* Why...?
I... I have to go back...
(Ægirian) Stop right there, Ægir.
Uh, y-yes!
The last Ægir of Gran Faro. The last descendant of Breogan.
Breo... what?
The Sea Terrors would kill you, and the Inquisition would imprison you. Come with me. I need you.
A-Are you one of them? The ones commanding the monsters?
No! I seek a lost legend, one that caught the attention of even the Academy.
Yet his most important legacy is neither a lighthouse nor a ship, but his descendant.
You might be the final clue I'm seeking.
I... don't know what you're talking about.
That man, Thiago, is not your father. Who were your parents? Your grandparents?
My parents...
They... they went missing... in search of the Eye of Iberia.
As cruel as it is, your misfortune is what gives you the chance to become the salvation of Ægir.
Come with me. I'd prefer not to use force against kin.
I... This is all coming out of nowhere.
Come with me.
Hmph.
What?! What's going on?
Quiet, bro. I'm saving your life.
Let's go.
A smoke bomb... I shouldn't have let you get away.
What's the point of running away with him? You know nothing, Liberi.
You're right, Ægir. There are many mysteries here.
Transgressor, you will answer my questions, or you will never leave this place.
I hope you're smart enough to recognize this fact.
Good, looks like we've shaken him off. It was the right choice to grab you when the Inquisitor drew near. They aren't fighting right now, are they?
Hello?
Oh, er, sorry... I need to go back to town.
Wouldn't recommend it. Most of the Sea Terrors are gone, but something doesn't feel right. And I can't get in touch with Dr. Kal'tsit.
Plus... you were planning to leave, weren't you?
I heard that the Inquisitors came to town, and Uncle Thiago was worried I'd be taken away...
The Inquisition, huh...
What did that guy want with you, anyway?
Sorry, you don't have to talk if you don't want to.
I'll need a bit of time to decide whether to go back to town and find Dr. Kal'tsit, or get you out of here first.
I'll go with you.
You sure? Your uncle was so desperate to get you out.
If the Inquisition is coming, I doubt I'll be able to escape.
Is that duty worth giving up your life for?
Huh? What do you—?
So you're going back to Gran Faro just out of some vague sense of duty, without even a clear goal?
You've gotta be kidding me. If I were you, I'd leave this place, along with any townsfolk who don't want to get involved. And soon.
This isn't like running into a swarm of Originium Slugs while camping out in the woods. I hate to say it, but ordinary people can't do a thing here.
I just feel like there has to be something left for me to do.
I grew up here, listening to those legends, gazing at the coastline, reading my parents' notes... I don't want my farewell to be like this.
This place is my home.

lines.st: 'I arrived in this new land from my maritime home. There is no end to ghastly shock here, not a break in the tragic weave.'
'The clouds are far from as fine as told in legend, and both the land and sky are wrathbitten and hurting. Cities comparable to Ægir are all but prey to Catastrophe.'

'Moreover, the people of this dry land are plagued by an inscrutable sickness. I had learnt of Oripathy, of Originium, from many an academy record. Seeing it firsthand, though, the truth of how it has become an integral part of the land, its roots deep and stubborn, is made witheringly apparent.'
'Nevertheless, they still tenaciously live, pursue both technology and truth through their unique means, and seek to leave the mark of their continued existence.'

'Though I am lambasted a traitor, I do not regret my decision to come ashore. The Seaborn swarm is expanding; within a decade, Ægir will find itself besieged with no quarter. But someday, I will complete my travels and return to Iberia.'

'I will leave this memoir behind, for the Ægir to seek and decipher. I have no faith in the nobility; I am certain that those Ægir termed 'Islanders' today will find Iberia withdrawing its respect for them soon enough, whatever the reasons may be.'
'The Golden Dreadnought, the Eye of Culture, the Jurisprudence of Evolution, the Stele of Life. The Ocean and Land must form a united front to withstand the billowing waves.'
'It was never my intent to serve as a pioneer. All I am is one step ahead of fate.'

lines.x: —From a copy of the notes of Breogan, famed Iberian architect and chief shipwright, written in cryptic Ægirian.
This is a matter between the Ægir.
So long as you stand on Iberian soil, the law is watching you. And so am I.
Hmph...
What is the relation between you and those three Abyssal Hunters?
You've harmed Skadi. Strange. Surely she intended to avoid combat.
Her blood is a special sort, more special than the rest of ours. I hope you've been diligent in cleaning your weapons.
I'm the one asking questions here.
And I'm the one who decides whether to answer.
I gather the only Hunters granted freedom of movement by Lord Carmen were those who declared their interests during the Sal Viento conflict.
...Mm. Seems it wasn't a coincidence you crossed swords with her then.
She was hardly weak.
...Weak?
What a naive appraisal. She's just unaccustomed to dry land. She lacks flexibility.
Don't move, Ægir.
Comply with the High Inquisitor's investigation. The Inquisition has already concluded negotiations with the Hunters. Your every move merely undermines the basis of our cooperation.
...Cooperation?
I advise you to rethink your actions.
Inquisitor, how many soldiers like you does Iberia have?
That is none of your business.
Enough to amass an army three thousand strong?
Then Iberia is still unqualified.
You're going nowhere!
(Sharp chafing sounds)
What? Where did they come from?
High Inquisitor! Are you alright?
I'm fine. That Ægir...
This was my fault, sir!
He could easily have used that chance to take an arm from me, yet he chose to flee.
It seems we can't afford to give chase just yet.
They're headed for the city! They're hiding in the soil!
No.
This entire swathe of earth has been polluted.
H-How can that be?
Take a good look at that gray land, and the sinister miasma filling it.
Yes, sir!
Gran Faro is not being infiltrated by the Church of the Deep. It has already fallen to them.
Prepare to fight head-on. Ensure Lord Carmen is safe, ASAP.
This fluorescent nethersea brand... It's akin to their own city.
It beggars belief. They're born from it, they proliferate and migrate through it...
(Ominous slithering sounds)
Not all lives thirst for independence. It's no wonder these filthy things were able to hide in this town.
...But who brought them ashore?
Welp, this is just perfect. And here we thought we could find a little inspiration on our beach trip. Talk about a total wash.
(Riffs in agreement)
The sea's caressing the land. I can tell. This is bad gone badder.
Kal'tsit once said the Ægir won, and that the Abyssal Hunters are the orphans of that battle. But with how things look now, I'm starting to doubt there was ever a win.
Oh, speaking of Kal'tsit, did she give us the thing?
Yep.
Way to beat around the bush. Why didn't she just give it straight to that Ægir? Gladiia. That would've been faster.
(Riffs in doubt)
She wanted to use the key to draw us into this. And from that view, she definitely succeeded.
Or maybe, she was originally planning to make us retrieve or help out with something. And then she changed her mind.
Huh? Hasn't she always been, like, three hundred steps ahead? What could possibly force her to change her mind?
...I'm just saying it's possible. Sure, she knows a lot of things, but more than that, she's cautious. If not, how many times would she have died by now?
Uh, what are you dancing around for? Dan?
This chapel would work great for a concert!
Skadi, Shark.
We wiped out every enemy we could see.
...Formless wisps of waves are spreading here, as the tide on sandy shores.
An inauspicious omen. We—We should not be here.
It seems cleaning up some trash wasn't enough to bring her back to her senses.
What do we do?
Once this area is spotless, we borrow the Iberian's assistance in finding that ship.
There are things we don't recognize on their bodies.
Perhaps a part of the Sea Terrors themselves.
...I've been wondering for a while now.
How can they still evolve so fast? How are they still so full of life?
Even though we've already...
That's precisely the question we intend to shed light on. It is also the reason I was willing to cooperate with Kal'tsit.
Dr. Kal'tsit...
A fine woman, to be sure.
Rouse yourself, Hunter. End this before the sun sinks below. Sea Terrors who cannot hear the ocean's call are but inferior lifeforms.
Understood.
You... Gladiia? You've come. I will listen, then.
...Shark.
Do you find it unwieldy in your hands?
This? I often forget the weapon is in my grasp. As if I'm dancing with it.
That is your past, and it has almost caught up to you.
We'll be waiting.
(Shuddering gargles)
You lead a punctual march, Dario. Have you arranged what I asked of you?
Yes, Lord.
The Penal Battalion has readied a ship to leave for the Eye of Iberia. They've also evacuated the coast and surrounded this town by now.
Those misguided fools have nowhere to flee.
There's nothing to fear in those petty villains, but the new threat they've brought forth is an astonishing one.
Our technology is able to treat this much pollution.
Which is only a temporary victory, Dario. And this is 'Ægir technology,' not ours.
If for every second of every minute, every droplet and every grain of the tides beating against our sandy shores spreads the Sea Terrors and their ecology, how are we to defend our homeland then?
We will find an answer before that happens.
Yes, most assuredly.
The Inquisition is also prepared to do battle with the Abyssal Hunters. We will throttle them to death on this remote shoreline.
A fitting cemetery. We can only hope they make the wise choice, and that Kal'tsit can accomplish what she's pledged to.
So this is your apprentice, then. I'm familiar with her from her work.
Y-Yes! Inquisitor Irene, reporting in, Lord Carmen!
They say that when Dario found you in the ruins, a thick tome of classics had lodged itself in the beams of the caving roof. A miraculous equilibrium that saved your life.
Yes, sir!
Young one, you are here only because Dario has determined that you have the will to rush onto the frontline.
Yes, sir!
There is an immaturity to you, but you are eager to prove yourself. Your body is filled with vigor and tenacity.
But your duties in Gran Faro will be no easier than in Sal Viento. From here on, you will only be plunging ever deeper into the heart of evil. Are you prepared?
For Iberia's orderliness and integrity, I shall raise my sword and my lantern.
To me, this motto is more than just a rallying cry.
She will be prepared.
Mm... I trust your judgment, but fate does not often give its people time to prepare.
Dr. Kal'tsit and I have matters to attend to still. The mission of guiding the Abyssal Hunters back to the Eye of Iberia is now yours.
The Eye of Iberia... High Inquisitor, what exactly is our task?
...Establish a camp, safeguard the route, and ensure that our maintenance teams can safely reach the lighthouse.
The last piece of the Eye of Iberia is fifty nautical miles out from the nearest coast to Gran Faro.
Assuming, of course, that it's still where the maps indicate.
Fifty nautical miles...
Once, our fleet could gaze far across the surface of the tranquil sea. But since the Profound Silence, the ocean has become like a boiling poison, melting all who touch it.
This is a daunting mission. But recapturing this final lighthouse is of tremendous importance to our people.
U-Understood, sir!
It is for this that we require the Ægirs' assistance.
You are skeptical.
She has been to see the Inquisition's underground cages.
So, you have witnessed the truth of the Inquisition's search, in spite of the fact it cannot obtain an answer.
Go, young Inquisitor. Time will not wait. But if necessary, I will have you put time itself on trial.
(Pronounced slithering sounds) —!
Watch out!
You okay there?!
I-I'm fine. Sorry, Mr. Elysium, I'm being a drag on us again...
Uh, haha, don't you mind it.
Those Sea Terrors...
(Identical wounds, pounded into holes in twos and threes. This style has to be Skadi and the girls.)
This town's been wrecked through and through.
Haha... yeah...
Whoa—hold it! Jordi!
Ngah!
Wh-What is this?
—Fluorescent... creepers? Floating creatures? Or is it... uh, coral?
This stuff is covering the whole street ahead... Hold on, something's coming out of the roofs—
—! Move!
Wait, wait—
—No way, they already have nests here?! I thought the Church of the Deep guys just sicced them on the town!
(And wait, could they seriously have just been born? This many?! How fast does it go?)
Jordi!
Oh, I'm here, I'm here!
Ditch! Take a 180 and run for it!
But they're behind us too, Mr. Elysium!
—Ugh, these monsters build nests faster than the freaking undertide gloompincers did—
Then it's do-or-die. Hey, Jordi, find something, anything. You gotta weaponize.
W-Will an umbrella do?
...Yeah, sure looks like we're in for some rain. Hold onto that thing like you mean it!
(Pronounced slithering sounds)
—! Get ready!
(Self-satisfied braying)
—Burn it all. Together with the buildings.
(Delighted hisses)
It would seem Ægir technology isn't necessary to treat the nethersea brand.
Dr. Kal'tsit!
Good, you're in one piece. What happened?
...And who is this somewhat despondent-seeming Ægir?
Outstanding strength, Hunters.
Spare us your remarks. Let us hasten to the topic at hand.
Kal'tsit has recently suffered quite the ordeal on your behalf.
The aid she has given us will be forever carved into my memory. While what you've done... I will likewise never forget.
But this present situation is by her arrangement. A small coastal town, a simple meeting.
Then in honor of her efforts, may we set aside our grievances and begin the discussion?
What you seek is Breogan's legacy. You want to find the Stultifera.
(Stultifera Navis, the 'Ship of Fools?' Wasn't that the flagship of the fleet wiped out in the Profound Silence? But how many years has it been?!)
What most piques my curiosity is how a foreigner can be so sure that it remains afloat.
Then, from the Iberian point of view, do you believe it to be sunk or not?
Hmm...
In all likelihood... it isn't.
However, it has not once returned to port.
A ship, unsunk for over sixty years, yet never returning to dry land's harbor.
Can your people determine its position?
We could once. But not now.
In other words...
Gladiia looks to the statue beside them.
She has no recollection of such buildings. Perhaps they had already died their silent deaths before she came ashore.
The 'Eye of Iberia.'
These wonders were once the cornerstone of a magnificent project, but most of the lighthouses that form the Eye perished in the Profound Silence. Only one remains.
It does not belong to you. It belongs to Ægir.
...Breogan was indeed an Ægir, and he came from the ocean, but his devotion was selfless. He made ample contribution to Iberia's sea explorations.
After the disaster, you let your citizens pour their rage upon the Ægir—upon him. Indulging such folly paints you in an untrustworthy light.
Yet are the Ægir able to swear no implication in the Profound Silence?
Walls will be the death of us all. If the two of you are so determined to continue this conversation, then both Iberia and Ægir are as good as destroyed.
Civilizations do not die, they are merely endlessly replaced. But the next time it happens, humanity will have no hand in it.
Dr. Kal'tsit, please mind your tongue with Lord Carmen.
I do not mind; we've certainly known each other long enough. But is this your usual method, Kal'tsit?
Surgically extracting the key individuals from the gargantuan body of fate, sitting them down in front of you, and convincing them of the rightness of your idea?
My idea is simply to save you. Iberia is free to refuse, as is Ægir.
If we count the untested young lady, then there are six at the table: three each for both the Inquisition and the Hunters.
The town square itself is an Iberian marvel constructed by an Ægir. It could not be more appropriate for such a conference.

At this very moment, the nethersea brand is still spreading. The Sea Terrors have built a nest in town, and their existence takes a vastly different form than before. A difference drastic enough to cause alarm.

If none present yet recognize the scale of this oceanic threat, then perhaps we really should adjourn. We can watch as the precious time bought by the sacrifice of the Abyssal Hunters is whiled away for nothing.
Was our victory... meaningful?
Of course. Without it, then after the Profound Silence, Iberia would not even have a deathbed to struggle upon.
As for you, Lord Carmen, I hope you can put aside your prejudices against the Ægir. Use your—
—I'm so very old now, Kal'tsit. Let's get to the point, shall we?
I've aged past the inclination to win every argument, as well as the strength to uphold glory through speeches.

There is no greater glory than survival, and no one sees the situation clearer than I. You may rescind your words. The Inquisition is willing to fully cooperate with the Abyssal Hunters in finding the lost dreadnought.
The golden... dreadnought.
I have no objections.
I would've thought contact between Ægir and Iberia would give you pause.
Contact? Ah... if you could breathe underwater, if you could visit our cities without the aid of equipment, then I would not mind showing you a true display of Ægir's technology.
We look forward to it, Madame Consul.
A Tercio of the Penal Battalion will immediately shore several dual-use warships at Gran Faro, establish a camp, and clean up the harbor in preparation.
Dario, Irene, go with the Abyssal Hunters. Prioritize securing a sea route to the Eye of Iberia. Pass your judgment on arrival.
As you command.
Kal'tsit, should...
Should time be of the essence, I will prioritize reestablishing contact with Ægir.
A voyage to the deep sea is a treacherous affair to us all, but for you, it will simply be a trip back home.
You will find that ship, and you will find a way back to Ægir. I leave the details to your discretion.
Perhaps it will be far in the future when next we meet.
So long as you remember our words. We hope that once you've returned, you will be able to reevaluate all that Ægir has suffered.
Then let us make haste. We will—
Ah—
Sorry, Dr. Kal'tsit. Uh, ever since the part about the lighthouse, Jordi's been—
The Ægir, perfect. I have much to ask you.
Wait.
Who are you?
I... I'm a resident of this town, my name is Jordi.
There shouldn't be any Ægir in Gran Faro! Our dossier says there are records of collusion between the Ægir and the Church of the Deep!
Who are your parents?
My... my parents were lighthouse technicians. They gave their lives for the Eye of Iberia.
My house is filled with dusty blueprints, I... I may have never actually seen the lighthouse, but...
I want to. The meaning of my homeland... the meaning of my parents' lifelong struggle... I, uh...
You may not. The Inquisition must first interrogate you.
And why should I consider protecting a feeble civilian in a melee?
What if he were a descendant of Breogan?
What? But he looks nothing like the photographic records of Breogan.
Even if it's true, we should be demanding he turn over what's left of his family's legacy, and let the Inquisition take over the research.
High Inquisitor!
What is your name, Ægir?
...Jordi. Jordi Fontanarossa. My uncle told me that—
Then you should know what awaits you for voluntarily appearing before the Inquisition.
I... I know.
Irene.
Yes, sir!
Seize him.
...And take him with you.
Yes... Er, what?
That was what you wished for, was it not?
Thank you, Lord. Nevertheless, my greatest hope is to see true, enduring cooperation between Ægir and Iberia.
Even the longest journey starts with a single step.
Then we don't have a moment to lose. We will let the Eye of Iberia gaze upon the desolate sea once again.
Eugh... What is this—disgusting floor? What's happening?
Is it the Sea Terrors?
Thiago.
...Amaia.
What's going on? Didn't you say those Church of the Deep cultists wouldn't show up? They've made a complete mess of the place.
Thiago, I came to say goodbye.
What?
It's about time for me to, and the opportunity is hard to come by now. That's all it is.
I don't understand.
Thiago.
The Inquisitors are here. A whole three of them.
Jordi's in their hands.
Gah—!
Don't, Thiago. Jordi came back by his own will. And what can you do against three Inquisitors?
Does he know what it means to be found by the Inquisition?!
You've told him so many times that I'd be shocked if he didn't.

But that's how people are. Reason might tell us to take every advantage and avoid the opposite, but people are so complicated, and you can't judge that so easily. I wouldn't call Jordi a fool for his choice.
Now's not the time to argue over morality—!
This is his nature we're talking about. Character, not morality, Mayor Thiago.
There's a certain beauty in human nature. Even time can't deny that.
What are you getting at?
Do you hate the Inquisition?
The Sea Terrors have blanketed this town, but remember, Thiago, remember.
Remember that rainy night, when the Penal Battalion charged into town, breaking down doors. The unending screams drowned out the pitter-patter of the rain.
No, stop...
And now?
The sea's brand is spreading across town—have you heard any screams? Have you seen anyone being senselessly slaughtered, or hunted down?
You haven't.
Right now, all that remains in Gran Faro—
—is stillness.
I never knew... that the ocean was so vast.
The sea breeze blows past.
Jordi cannot believe what's before his eyes. He had always thought the sea's horizon was the sky's end.
Not until witnessing this boundless expanse of waves for himself does Jordi—the Ægir—have a sudden question.
Why has humanity never desired to explore this?
...How could we ever determine our position out here? It's not as if the moons are signal beacons, or the stars could be read like a map.
Um, why can't we read the stars like a map?
Ask a professional astronomer and find out.
Then... if Ægir really is under the water, then how big is it? Bigger than Iberia?
There's a handful of genuine Ægir over there. How about you ask them?
They seem like they have their own worries, though. They're all so solemn.
Shark, what are you listening to?
...The wind.
There's an offering of kin mingled with the sea breeze. It puts me... much at ease.
She gets more like this the closer we are to the sea.
Even though in Sal Viento, she was so...
Do you know why?
I could guess, but it would only disturb your judgment. We have a battle before us.
Did you ever attempt to return to the sea, after your narrow escape to shore?
Of course. I failed.
There was no end to them. They surrounded me. I ran out of strength before I could even swim far.
I tried so, so many times.
But you're still alive.
We are all well aware of the changes in our bodies, Skadi. You included.
Shark may have suffered some injury, but none of my Hunters are so fragile.
And she's the same as us.
'Specter.' Once a victim of the Church of the Deep. Originium, absent in the ocean, has affected her in a whole host of ways.
Why bring along such an uncertain element?
We are going home. It does not concern you.
Home? But circumstances in Ægir can hardly be better than in Iberia.
Raise your lantern, Inquisitor. Let's prepare to greet our first enemies.
Oh. They've come.
One final question.
Are there really only three Abyssal Hunters?
If there were others, I would have long since found them.
...What compels you to ask?
(Ominous slithering sounds)
Is this ship's course true?
Undoubtedly.
...Then the Sea Terrors are becoming more explicitly hostile as we approach the Eye of Iberia.
Perhaps it is already their nest. Fighting them aboard this ship would waste far too much time.
Skadi, Shark, defend this vessel. Keep an eye on the primitive signal navigation facilities while you're at it.
I will enter the sea.
I'll go with you.
You are stronger in the sea, but so are they. And we cannot bleed down there; the risk is too high.
Wh-What's wrong?! Are there monsters attacking again?
No, I can't see any! We don't know what's happening under the water, but—
That muffled booming! What's going on down there? Is she fighting below?
No.
She's just... moving. She's faster in the water.
...How can anyone move faster through water than air?
Habit, I guess. Or maybe it's a skill.
The Sea Terrors are following her. She's opened up a passage.
Irene, take Breogan's descendant and continue to steer.
Pick up speed before the enemy surrounds us.
They've left. The seas won't be peaceful.
If you worry for them, we'd best hurry and finish up our business.
Gran Faro is only an outpost to recapture the lighthouse. Retaking it is all that matters.
I'm surprised that Iberia can already exploit Ægir technology to resist the encroaching nethersea brand.
Regardless, the rate of its evolution is faster than any one scientist's genius. If we seek victory, we must use whatever we can.
Even an army, I see.
...This is an plain, unremarkable town.
Perhaps there are more strategic locations waiting for the Inquisition to recapture them, one by one. More favorable battlefields, grander walls and fortifications.
But, sometimes, it's fine to start with what's at hand.
However many Iberians are willing to fight to the death against the waves, that is how many soldiers we can field.
Let us begin the cleanup.
(Unabated slithering sounds)
Hey... Are we still not there yet?!
A-According to the nautical map, we should be able to at least see it by now...
High Inquisitor! We have to head deeper in, or else—
No.
Look up.
What? It's nothing but black overhead, I can't see—
No... that's not... that's not the sky...
They've made it their nest by now. Very close to the shore; no wonder their numbers are so great.
We can just cut them all down. Like before.
...Yes, like before.
Shark?
So that is... their eye? Why does it feel so familiar?
Mm.
It has been caked in filth for too long, unable to open its lid.
I'll clear a path. Jordi, come with us.
Ah. O-Okay... I hope I can be of help...
No. There's something only you can do.
If you can't, then we'll have to change strategies and prioritize ensuring that the Inquisition's technicians can make it here safely.
And in the battle to come, we'll have no time to care for the fate of a solipsistic Ægir.
Got it!
You seem nervous.
Ægir. You don't understand what finally reaching this island again signifies for us.
And what would that be?
...Ever since the Penal Battalion discovered remnant signals from the lighthouse, we've made seventeen attempts to make landfall, eight of which succeeded in setting foot on this reef.
Hundreds of soldiers, and three Inquisitors. No one returned alive.
Don't tell me your superior Ægir ethics teach you to respect your heroes, but forsake their memory?
The High Inquisitor and I... the Iberians will hold a memorial for those who died at sea.
I did not mean to pick at your morals. All I can say is that, in the face of the ocean, we are all alone and weak.
Do as you wish, but we are pressed for time.
A foot falls on the reef.
A colossus hides in the jet-black winds. Only by tilting one's head back and gazing far up can one distinguish it from the murky sky.
The Eye of Iberia.
A final pair of eyes, sealed shut.
So tall...
The scale of that statue in town has to be wrong...
High Inquisitor.
Mm.
This is what we once...
It is... my first time seeing it as well.
The two remain silent for a moment, dead in their tracks as they look over at this vast spectacle that's survived only in documents and records.
The enduring reality of Iberia goes far beyond what she was taught, Irene thinks. Despite the Abyssal Hunters' ample disdain for them, despite the balance of the nation well and truly crumbling.
Despite it all, this lighthouse is an emblem. Of past glory and wild ambition. Of dreams and the domination of the unknown.
Whatever feelings may roil in your heart at this moment, engrave them all.
This building, these worn swords and lanterns, these reefs, this ocean.
Carve into your heart just what humanity has built in its struggle. Keep it with you forever, at all times.
If we do not venerate our sacrifices, our scales are meaningless.
Yes, sir!
Enter carefully.
Got it!
What are you doing?
Have we come home?
No, not yet.
But if we find what Kal'tsit and the Captain are looking for here, maybe we can.
Just hold on... we're almost there.
It's too quiet.
But it still stinks.
Yes. The reef is coated in remnant nethersea brand. I can see traces of man-made objects too, rotted for the most part.
Gladiia paces slowly. She looks up at the tower, standing against the tenebrous sky.
The Eye of Iberia is directed towards the heavens, as if upon this finite soil, life cranes its neck, wanting to see the future.
The nations of the land have done all they can to reproduce Breogan's remaining legacy. These so-called lighthouses are an emulation of Ægir technology through and through.
What can this lighthouse be awaiting, sat in this void and empty sea?
Does it watch the ocean, or the land, I wonder.
Captain.
I know. Within is a Sea Terror nest. The lack of an immediate attack may merely be defensive, a guarding of the den.
It is about time they come.
(Ominous slithering sounds)
There's not... too many.
The sea. The coast is already out of sight.
Sentimental?
No... I just mean, we can't bleed here. It'll be extremely dangerous.
They can't pierce our skin. So long as you do not cease your dance, these numbers are insufficient to kill us.
(The lighthouse... It really is just like the statue in town.)
(Which means, those blueprints at home really were from my mother and father. For the Eye of Iberia...)
(My parents were once... in this very place.)
Jordi looks back towards the shore.
Each and every day, his mind went back to his view of that overcast shore. A lifeless gray, a hopeless crowd.
But, ah, look at this lighthouse, he thinks.
Uncle Thiago was right. They were all heroes. They were all meant to be legends.
...The Abyssal Hunters should be regrouping not far from here. They will be joining this battle too.
Don't forget what you experienced in Sal Viento. Their blood is highly unusual, and Kal'tsit hardly told us everything.
U-Understood. So they can't be trusted.
What did you see, beneath the Inquisition?
—Erk.
'Seaborn.'
You should still remember their twistedness, their vitality, their... serenity.
In Sal Viento, the Hunters did battle such a monster, and emerged victorious.
I... I know that.
You've seen the truth, so now you must reassess your past, and Iberia.
Tear apart the old, swimming through your head! Make your own judgments.
Y-Yes, sir!
—(Rough sounds of slithering over reef)—
They're ready for us. Withdrawn to defend their nest, waiting for the chance to strike. Here to drive out the invaders.
Jordi. Report?
R-Right! It's a miracle, really. These control panels outside are still responding, but I'm not sure what theory these things are based on...
I-I'm looking though! I have all those research notes from home with me in my bag!
...Be quick about it.
I know! It's this one... No, that's not it... Eek, when did this one get wet?
High Inquisitor! They're here!
The billows rage forth.
Their arrogance surges dozens of meter high, shattering one day after another, succumbing to the reef and the three moons.
One moon is bright. One moon is dark. These are the moons spoken of throughout time, hung in the sky.
The remaining moon comes from the intervals betwixt the waves, at the peaks of every crest.
The moonlight comes from the title he once believed in.
*yawn*... Is it morning already?
The operators' equipment was a lot more seriously damaged than we anticipated... Still, the fact that they managed to save so many people from that Catastrophe is a testament to how amazing they are.
Mhm. I've got to chin up as well.
Hi Maria, I'm here to pick up an order from the Engineering Department.
Oh, Grani! Just a moment. Let's see... where did I put that? Sorry, I haven't tidied this place up for a few days, so it's a bit of a dump.
Ahaha... It sure looks more like a proper Kazimierzian workshop than the last time I stopped by. Huh? What's this?
A novel?
'The Last Knight'! What's that doing here? Did it get stuck underneath the blueprints when I was flipping through my boxes and drawers yesterday? I was wondering where it had gone.
'The Last Knight'?
It used to be my favorite book. My sister gave it to me as a gift when I was little.
A knight novel from Kazimierz, huh?
Mind lending it to me?
Sure thing!
Ugh, so that's where the book on Arts Unit theory ended up... Sorry Grani, this might take a while. I can bring it over to you later, if you like.
Don't worry, it's not urgent. Just means I get to stay on Rhodes Island a bit longer this time.
I'll just be over here reading, to pass the time.
I'm really, really sorry about this.
'The Last Knight.'
The title is as pompous as it is cliché. There have probably been hundreds of books written about the last knight, the swiftest knight, or the strongest knight.
But seeing how the Radiant Knight gifted it to her own sister, surely there must be more to it than that?
With that thought, Grani opens it up, only to find a bookmark wedged inside.
Written in wobbly Kazimierzian letters is a line that may well have been copied from the book.
'If you deem the waves too raucous, then command the ocean to be still.' Huh...?
Legend has it that the Last Knight returned to Iberia. The enemies reflected in his eyes were not living creatures, but rather, he charged at the mountains, the cities, and the waves.
Legend has it that the Last Knight settled by the coast, sustaining himself on the wind and taking shelter in the mist, until at last he forgot how to remove his helmet.

Legend has it that the Last Knight finally disappeared after clashing against the great waves. His family discovered what he'd left behind along the shore and brought it back to the Land of Knights, burying it deep in the mountains.

Legend has it that his or her story was brought to light by a poet. Embellished, sung, given both meaning and symbolism. And in this form, they did finally return to the Land of Knights, as a highly controversial figure.
However...
...There are some who believe that the mad knight triumphed over the ocean, over time, over death itself. That his quest continues until the end of all things.
What... are you?
The knight does not answer. His deathly gaze is fixed on Gladiia.
However, she quickly realizes that he is not actually looking at her.
He stares only at the key in her hands.
Guh... *cough* *cough*... Hiss...
The knight opens his mouth as if to speak. But only a shriveled rasp comes forth, like the sound of muscle tearing.
He begins to take a step forward.
Where did you get that key?
Oh, I did not intend to... pry into Rhodes Island's internal affairs.
But that is Breogan's legacy, passed through the hands of the four Feranmuts to once again return to that Abyssal Hunter.
By pure coincidence, one of the Hunters found it in Kazimierz.

...Kazimierz? What a distant land. I still remember journeying with my mentor to receive an envoy from the Land of Knights, and secretly overhearing a few Silverlance campaign knights describing their ideals.
I hear the country has changed considerably since then.
For better or worse, every political entity has undergone similar changes in our current era. But that has nothing to do with Iberia.
We are like ensnared beasts. Should we struggle, we suffer and bleed. Should we give up, our lives are forfeit.
Before we unmask those still-human traitors, I have a new question for you, Kal'tsit.
No doubt you've already realized this, but that child couldn't possibly be Breogan's descendant. The Inquisition would not have missed such a vital clue.
So why would you still condemn him to a meaningless death?
(Calls out to its kin with a slithering sound)
High Inquisitor!
...Their numbers are increasing. But, none of them showed up until we reached the lighthouse.
S-Sir, I've found a way to open the main door!
But the Eye of Iberia's core facilities are at the top of the tower. And it's very, very tall! The lift mechanism isn't working either, so we can only get up there by foot!
(Slithering sounds)
High Inquisitor, we need more time!
Take the Ægir with you.
There is no time to spare, Irene.
Aye! Right away!
Wait, hold on!
(Intense slithering sounds)
Oh, are they here? No, the lantern's flames are flickering. Something has stepped into my Arts' domain.
(Cowering slithering sounds)
(They're scared. Strange, these things have never shown signs of fear before.)
(Confusion then? But what could be the cause...?)
(Confused slithering sounds)
The High Inquisitor raises his lantern high, and the Sea Terror in turn retreats into the shadows of the reef.
When he looks around once more, he notices the reef is littered with corroded and broken weapons, soaked with the fresh blood of monsters.
Arms belonging to the Inquisition. As his mentor Carmen had said, it is not as if the Inquisition had never considered reclaiming the sole remaining lighthouse.
Being unaffected by the Profound Silence, it's been quite well-preserved.
Even so, the Inquisition has never made a serious attempt to retrieve its own Eye following the Profound Silence.
This place used to be a spectacular overlook. A breathtaking, unobstructed view of the entire city.
It had been manned by Iberians, day in, day out.
(Cautious slithering sounds)
But you've taken it as a feeding ground, building a nest for your spawn without permission.
Hup...
Emotions.
Long-lost emotions.
From soldier to Inquisitor, and finally to one who sees the truth. He has been tempered, lost and reforged anew countless times. And thus, Dario became one of the few High Inquisitors in Iberia.
Only a single word separates an Inquisitor from a High Inquisitor, but that single word is the difference between heaven and earth.
He used to regard himself as resolute as steel. Before Iberia's enemies, his lantern would flicker, his sabre would glimmer, and every line of the Scriptures would be put into practice.
But now, he feels a type of strong emotion welling up within him. One not solely a product of the law and justice that is preached.

After acknowledging its rationality, High Inquisitor Dario accepts the fact that he is truly experiencing grief and anger. His handcannon takes aim at the Sea Terror, yet his eyes are directed towards the sea.
When you die, mankind may thrive.
These words are not a part of our laws or scriptures, but rather yours. 'Survival of the fittest.'
Right here!
It's just as the records say! The lighthouse relies on the power source located at its base, and the core column is divided into several areas. The console on this floor controls—
...Controls what?
Uhh, signal recording.
We should be able to put that to use later. But for now, we need to prioritize powering the entire lighthouse. Let's continue.
Hopefully all goes well until reinforcements arrive.
Ugh—!
What's wrong, Doña Irene?
...It's nothing. Probably just my imagination. Maestro... the High Inquisitor is keeping guard outside. We'll be fine.
A-Alright... After we restart the lighthouse's power system, we should be able to use the lift as well. That way the others will be able to catch up to us soon enough.
So let's hurry!
There's something odd about him. Some kind of... lingering scent.
But what is it?
The scent of another land. Nothing you'd be interested in.
He is without a doubt a Seaborn. But not the same as Quintus.
How is it possible for so many land creatures to become Seaborn though? They shouldn't have frequent contact with the ocean.
With no way to communicate, we can do nothing but let his body lose all function.
He's about to attack!
The knight suddenly seems to vanish in place. But for the senses of an Abyssal Hunter, catching him is hardly a challenge at all.
With his speed, he opts not to attack them, but instead leaps high into the air and bounds toward the lighthouse with a ferocious roar.
Graaaaaagh!
Woah... Wh-What was that?
Don't panic! Keep climbing!
Complete our mission: rekindle this lighthouse!
R-Right!
(These vibrations... Are the walls coming under attack? How is that possible? We're already dozens of meters above ground. Do the Sea Terrors have some sort of cannon?)
(Or... maybe some massive creature? But other than the tremor just now, I haven't noticed anything else.)
(Maestro...!)
The knight lifts his head and looks up. The lighthouse did not budge an inch.
He shakes his twisted weapon disapprovingly. This long spear, once the creation of a master craftsman, is now inundated with the scent of the depths.
He does not see the one standing in his way. The knight bends his knees again, intending to try once more.
What are you doing?
You contemptuously provoke Iberia, trespass upon restricted areas, and damage a precious heritage site.
Just look at yourself. Do you have even a single shred of human-born dignity left in you?
...Dig...nity...?
Seems your vocal chords still work at least.
(Unintelligible sounds) ...The sea... doesn't need.
Crush... the waves. Trample the sun. End the pain.
Things should not, remain the same. The great tree, takes root in this land.
Your stance. Your gray armor. And that spear.
You're from Kazimierz?
I am... I am...
Knight!
...Slithering steel... Ocean's waves...
(Lifting the lantern won't do anything. There's no sign of any other ships coming.)
(The fluorescent nethersea brand ignored him. He may well be the source of the Sea Terrors' panic.)
(Then... is he actually a Seaborn disguised as a human?)
You seek to destroy the Eye of Iberia? The Inquisition will not allow it.
Aaaaaargh, the soil—!
Now!
...Good evening, Mister Knight.
Where are the other two?
The others? They're... currently having a little chat with Mister Knight's friends.
So a fight broke out not far away. Seems like it's a clash of civilizations then.
It has nothing to do with me, though, so I came over here.
You... You're different from how you were back in Sal Viento.
And also from how you were when we met in Gran Faro. You're always changing.
The seawater is sobering. Even the slightest breath of air brought in by the ocean breeze.
Although... I do want to return to the sea's embrace, but Skadi stopped me.
My little Skadi. She's so scared... But of what?
With what's going on right now, I need Gladiia's explanation all the more.
(Ægirian) Mister Knight, good sir, return to your sweet slumber, deep in the abyss.
(Ægirian) We are not enemies.
The knight ignores Laurentina, who murmurs as if singing.
As for the 'enemy' who'd just dealt him a heavy blow, the knight only tilts his head slightly before quickly snapping his attention back to the towering lighthouse.
Alas, how tragic. Language can no longer reach your ears, just as those clouds cover your eyes.
Even in your madness, does your will still drive you forward like clockwork, compelling your body to advance?
Come then, storm.
Let us share this dance.
I've found it! The main control panel!
How's it looking?
I, I'm not too sure. This is more complicated than what's on the diagrams, and, uh, I've never actually operated these things before.
That sound again... What's going on outside?
Perhaps we're still underestimating the dangers we might encounter here. At this rate, holding out for the Penal Battalion's technicians might be...
No, even if reinforcements arrive, the sacrifices required to repair the lighthouse under these conditions are akin to setting up camp under the crosshairs of a high-speed warship.
Jordi, you're a civilian, and a part of Iberia. For now, I won't ask where you came from, or about what happened in Gran Faro's past.
We're in a race against time. Right now, this instant, try your best to get all the information out of the Eye of Iberia as you can. Maestro and I will protect this tower, and we—
A-All right... I've managed to retrieve the logs. The panel is still responding, and the energy module underneath the tower is still functional.
...You act fast. Commendable.
What's next? How long would it take to reboot the entire lighthouse?
I, I'll see what I can do!
Um... Even though I know the people out there are fighting against those sea monsters, I still have to say this!

As long as the panel is still responding, it shouldn't be too hard to restart it. I've read about this in the notes my parents left behind... B-But, this would be my first time actually operating something like this, so it's still a bit of a gamble!
Either way, it all takes time! We should probably—
Eek—!
Guard this place, right?
Don't look down. Keep moving forward.
Can't wait any longer?
If what Kal'tsit said was true, then none of us can really afford to wait around.
Even while we walk this land, we are still a part of the sea. Those corrupted descendants are... in the process of changing the 'environment.'
In time, none of us will be spared. All we're doing now is sitting around waiting for the end to come.
We're not the only ones sitting around though, and everyone's reasons are different.
Think about it more broadly. Who knows, maybe these lands aren't suited for our survival?
(Riffs in agreement)
Not harmonious enough.
There's something I wanna try.
Time to mix it up, all for the tunes!
(Riffs in agreement)
So what exactly do you have in mind?
Uh... Well, we're not exactly accomplishing much over here.
Just treat it as a little pre-concert workout. Don't swim too far out, Frost.
A workout. Huh. What about the rest of you?
I think I'll pass.
Too lazy.
I can feel the inspiration on its way. It's so close. Just a liiittle bit more.
...Alright. (Riffs enthusiastically)
I'll be off then.
*pant*... *pant*...
No, what did that woman and the Inquisitor do? How could they so easily erase the gift of the sea?!
Ægir... It must've been the Ægir. What the Ægir left behind, those strange machines! They must be destroyed!
(Makes a slithering sound as it clings to the cultist's calves)
That's right, my brother, the Penal Battalion has this place surrounded, and we've failed to break through... We've nowhere to run.
But... our exalted prophet has returned to the sea.
All that's left... is to pave the way for Her.
(Impatient slithering sounds)
If they seek to hold back the tide, then let them dash against the rocks. My life is but one brick in a lengthy road.
Eek!
Don't panic, it's just me. Why haven't you taken refuge yet?
M-Mayor Thiago... I was drunk and out as cold as a gloompincer, so I didn't hear anything.
By the time I woke up, everything had gone to shit, and these disgusting things were all over the place.
What happened here? Has the Inquisition sent anyone yet? What's going to happen to us?
...The great ocean is not guilty of any crime, but the Inquisition does not agree. They control the so-called law, and have condemned us as sinners.
Soon enough, the Sea Terrors will return to the ocean. But I cannot allow the Inquisitors to condemn Gran Faro to death just like that.
What are you talking about? Have you gone mad?
Mad? I'm not the one who's mad! I simply need to know! What can we do before the Inquisition destroys this town and rounds us all up to stand trial?!
An Inquisitor has already arrived. I saw that clearly when hiding in a little alley. I've never seen one that old before, and they gave me the chills...
What are you gonna do then, Thiago? Go up to them and try to reason with them?
We'll have to choose sides again, right?
When the Ægir were taken away, most people looked on, just like that. They had a chance to stand up for them, but nobody stepped forward.
Thiago, you—
Now, we have another chance. Either watch the Inquisition put everyone in Gran Faro on trial, or—
Have you lost your marbles?! Inquisitor! Hey, Inquisitor! He can handle us all without raising a hand!
Take me to them.
...What are you talking about?
Drop the act. How long do you think I've lived in this town?
Alright, follow me. If your goal really is to take revenge on the Inquisitors...
Just what are you up to, Mayor Thiago?
Almost there.
This is already taking too long.
S-Sorry.
It's not your fault. It's mine, for not having enough patience.
Um... Doña Inquisitor, if you're worried about the others, you can go help them. I think I can handle things up here.
The High Inquisitor gave me a order to protect you. As you've seen, those monsters are everywhere. I can't just leave you alone.
But I don't want to be what's holding you back from joining the battle... I just—
As long as you can light up this tower, then the High Inquisitor will have made the correct decision. I simply defer to his judgment.
...I understand.
Did your knowledge of the Eye of Iberia come from your parents?
Yes. Or more precisely, it's the sum of all of Gran Faro's past. They gathered all the best lighthouse engineers in all of Iberia, or at least that's what my uncle told me.
The territory used to be demarcated by the Inquisition, until it was abandoned after a string of incidents involving the Ægir and the Church of the Deep.
I'm not trying to reopen old wounds, but I've been thinking. Before I left, I read the dossier about the sentence handed down at the time. ...No, never mind.
Do you hate the Inquisition?
Um... To be honest...
If there's anything that always hangs over the heads of the people back home, other than the ubiquitous dark clouds, it would be the word 'Inquisition.'

The town once glimmered, giving us the confidence to rebuild our homes. The workers and engineers gave everything they had to fight the disaster, but the Inquisition took everything before it even struck.
And do you still believe that today?
It's the place I grew up in. Everything I've ever seen or heard has only reinforced that lesson.
And, um, I'm sorry, ma'am.
You are simply stating your lived experiences, citizen. It is your actions that will prove your loyalty to Iberia.
...Still, I'm astonished you had the guts to say all that in front of an Inquisitor.
I never would've dared, not until I came here... But, then I saw the Eye. For the longest time, it's only appeared in my dreams, nothing more than a sculpture sitting in the plaza.
Everyone in Gran Faro who came before me has passed this all down. Our duty, what we must do...
It's all surging through me right now—
—Um, ma'am, do you see something glimmering at the base of the lighthouse? Something blue, and sparkling like stars...
That's—
—The nethersea brand!
Do you really think you can fight me, Mister Knight?
You only care about the lighthouse. Why? Why do you cling to it so?
Monstrous waves... Swallowing the sky...
...Even the stalk of wheat... consumed by silence. All things great and small... returned to nothing.
(Agitated slithering sounds)
He can speak, but not command his kin. The Sea Terrors are in disarray.
Let us put an end to this.
(Rasping roar) —!
—What a surprise, Mister Knight. You're not moving like you were before.
Those are human movements. A charging wind-up, unique to Kuranta knights. What exactly are you?
The knight continues to ignore his two opponents. His attention is solely on the third.
Even with his attack, the towering lighthouse does not even shake. If even the Profound Silence failed to destroy the Eye, what chance does a lone knight have?
It bewilders him. It pushes him to try even harder.
(Rasping murmur) Clouds, rolling hills, pillars of ash... arrayed across a hotbed of decay.
The tides... unending...
(Violent slithering)
You're injured, and the Sea Terrors are drawn to you.
So it appears. Got a little scratched up on accident—
Ngh...
Step aside. You're no longer fit to fight.
Dario's eyes quickly dart across the Abyssal Hunter in front of him. Her movements had paused unnaturally a few times during the previous clash.
He's heard reports about her. She was involved in the Sal Viento experiments, but the woman who stands before him is completely different from the one who emerged from that fierce battle.
Who was she? And who is she now?
They've hardly touched you, yet you seem barely able to stand.
Maybe an illness you contracted in Sal Viento?
...The scent of the sea is rather intoxicating, that's all.
...Knight ...Comrade. Why, why must you gag on the air, and endure the heat? You'll never taste... the fruits of civilization.
Urgh...!
The ground is shaking.
Energy long-lost floods through the pipelines and into the broken body of the Eye of Iberia, prying open its eyelids.
It has awakened. The entire island whispers the groans of its first light. Slowly it shakes off its winter, as if waking from centuries of hibernation.
...The cycle of all existence...
Does not belong to the sea...
The knight lifts his head, gazing up from the shadow beneath the great lighthouse.
He is perplexed.
That... is not the monstrous waves.
Where... are they?
Without drawing a single breath, as naturally as a shadow slants in the direction of the light, Specter brings down her weapon.
But the humanoid Seaborn in front of her avoids it, with logic-defying explosive force.
In midair, the knight looks far out into the distance.
He is listening, listening for the coming of new waves. And he will break them.
...Ishar... mla...
(Rasping growl) —Roci—nante—!
(Braying) —!!
(Unintelligible sounds) Chase!
Turns out, Mister Knight is afraid of the light.
Don't pursue him. He's not the only Seaborn like that out there. Our only duty is to defend this lighthouse.
He's only lost interest in the lighthouse.
I don't care about the motivations of monsters.
Are you saying... I'm also a monster?
You?
You are undergoing a transformation. I don't know whether this is something that happens with every Abyssal Hunter.
But if necessary, this reef will be the last bit of land you'll ever step on.
You would still call this land, when we're clearly surrounded by ocean?
Disasters change coastlines, not national boundaries.
Every last centimeter of land swallowed by the Profound Silence is still subject to the laws of Iberia.
Laws, a virtuous word. Yet not all creatures recognize this virtue.
...Ready your blade.
J-Just a little bit more!
(Slithering sounds)
Hurry up then! If this drags out any longer, the lighthouse will be swallowed up by the nethersea brand!
...Ran into a little bit of a hitch!
One of the auxiliary structures might've been damaged, b-but that would take too much time to investigate!
Just booting up the core facility will suffice. We still have to retrieve the flagship's coordinates, ideally reestablishing a connection with the line running along the coast—
I, I know, but— Those things might be even higher up!
Then keep going! Hurry!
I have to stay here... Turning on the lighthouse is as simple as pulling the most conspicuous lever, but I need to stay here and oversee the power system rebooting.
You should go, ma'am.
What? But if you're attacked by a Sea Terror that slipped through the net, we'll all—
I know! But I, I don't want to come up one step short, not now!
Those monsters— They only lashed out against humans because we invaded their nest, right?
If that's the case, they won't keep destroying the facilities. If anything, getting into a fight will only make things more dangerous, so you should keep climbing instead!
...A conspicuous lever, right?
Yeah, it should be obvious. Flip that, and at least the main facilities, including the lighting equipment, will start up again. ...Assuming what the log said is correct.
Alright.
I'll be right back, Jordi.
The console... Well, this is it.
Nothing on the outside is visible from within the tower.
Could the Stultifera really not have sunk? How are they supposed to find that great dreadnought?
Is it too late for the Penal Battalion to come to their aid? Are the cultists of Gran Faro hiding even more secrets?
Just by pulling down on lever in her hand, all of these questions would be answered.
Irene should have been conflicted. She had accepted too much, learned much of the sea, and seen it all with her own eyes.
—Now's the time.
I hope you're ready, Irene.
Dazzling light penetrates the ocean, piercing through everything.
The light will lead the way. The light can dispel the darkness.
It worked!
Hey, Jordi! Did you see that? We did it—
(Slithering sounds, rubbing against the wall)
Merda...
I didn't think you still had it in you to appreciate this sculpture.

The Eye of Iberia... The offspring of the marriage between Ægir and Iberian technology. On land, each nomadic city can only seamlessly communicate within its own walls. Between cities, however, is like crossing a moat.
No system of communication can resolve the interference caused by Originium-rich zones. The only solution is to set up base stations that are at constant risk of being destroyed by Catastrophes.
I hear some countries have already adopted the technology of mobile base stations that navigate on a fixed route between cities. Their engineers are no different from those who guard our lighthouses.
Even today, humans still stumble about across this great land, blindly grasping for clues. We must still grope our way across Terra in near total darkness.
But, what of the ocean?
There is no Originium in the ocean. The Ægir's communication technology vastly surpasses anything we have on land. The Eye of Iberia is not just a set of lighthouses.
It is, of course, Iberia's vision. Very few people know that it was left to us by Breogan, as a microphone to connect all of Iberia.
It's been a while, sir.
Your work was invaluable in allowing the Inquisition to respond so quickly to Dame Kal'tsit's proposal.
How about you? Have you enjoyed your time in Gran Faro?
Compared to the other cities where our colleagues lay dormant, this place can hardly be considered dangerous.

What did it find?
We don't have time—
*cough* I'll never get used to handcannons.
Hey, where are you—
Huh? The Abyssal Hunters?
Hello, Irene. I see you've brought us more prey.
Did you just call me Irene? Have you... have you regained your memories?
Not entirely, but I know I'm in a great mood right now.
This is not the time.
That's the one from the deck. It's fast enough to dodge the captain's lunge.
I really must tip my hat to you, Irene. For chasing it all the way here without taking a scratch.
I can't take credit for it. It hardly even attacked. Look, it's still not, even now.
I see... I thought it wouldn't be able to resist if we chatted like this and showed it an opening.
Your friend just left, you know. It's not too late to go after them.
Or are you a pacifist too?
(Kazimierz, a landlocked country where Skadi stayed for a time. A place of sprawling grasslands and forests, the Land of Knights.)
(Knights...)
Is it a coincidence? Or did something draw him there?
This ship, Breogan. What did you intend to leave behind for your homeland? Where have you hidden the answer?
...Hmm.
(Sea Terror carcasses? And not only that...)
(The wounds are clean and precise. They show no signs of resistance. Scents mingle in the air. The air here is stale.)
(What else is there on this ship?)
Your belt and pocket watch are undoubtedly human in origin, and even in the Iberian style!
What are you?
It's stalling for time.
This will get complicated if the other one gets back to the ocean.
Still not attacking? What a passive dance partner.
I'll take the lead, Skadi.
Right.
—Guh!
Guh... grrrr... gah...
Come on, fight back already. I'm starting to feel like the bad guy.
It's not stalling for time!

lines.st: Who defiles my Iberia? Heathens...

lines.x: The sound of bells?! What?
(Sharp screech)
Bells echo through the empty hall.
The lights in the hall flash. The Seaborn lands in the direction of the throne while the three are distracted.
Yes, the throne.
There is a throne on this Iberian ship. A symbol of pride, power, arrogance, and deceit.
It's like...
Specter trails off, knowing what she was about to say was far too absurd.
It wears a crown, and drapes itself in the same colors as the curtains. It kneels by the side of the throne, the two inextricably bonded with one another.
It resembles the queens and ministers of old, serving the golden sun of Iberia.
A silhouette emerges from behind the throne.
Irene raises her lantern over her head, trying to illuminate the shadowy figure.
Why do you set foot on my ship?
*cough* *cough* Pah.
Who would ignore a question from the great Alfonso? Why are you here?
Why have you broken the silence, and come for MY Iberia, which had lain forgotten for decades?
I will start with you. Answer me, child of Old Iberia.
Irene could not believe her eyes.
She'd seen the Seaborn's face before. She had devoted much time of her time during her Inquisitor training to the study of history, immersing herself in doctrines and creeds.
How dare this Seaborn claim to be Alfonso?
How dare it claim to be Iberia's greatest ship captain?
—You, you can't possibly be the captain of this ship, monster!
Alfonso was almost fifty years-old sixty years ago, but you look even younger than the great hero was back then!
Irene knows she is shaken, but she cannot show weakness.
She reflexively raises her lantern, as her commanding officer would have.
Drop the mask, Seaborn!
The light of her lantern flickers. The tiny speck in the Inquisitor's hand seems to fit the word 'lost' better than the lights of this golden hall.
The Golden Age.
The 'Seaborn' briefly lays its eyes on Irene before turning away dismissively, rubbing 'Alfonso's' inhuman arm.
A short silence surrounds the hero.
'Seaborn'? I don't like that name.
Tell me your name, child of Old Iberia.
I am Irene, Inquisitor of Iberia.
And 'Old Iberia'? You dare defile the name of—
And you dare talk to me like that?!
(A screech interrupts the exchange)
Agh!
Where were you when we burned Victoria's ships and sunk the Lion King's honor to the bottom of the lake?
Where were you when I paved the streets of Sal Viento with pure gold, brought back in my ships?
Where were you when the Tercios darkened Leithanien's dawn like a black cloud?
You—
(Violent coughing) *cough* *cough* Pah.
I am Alfonso, Duke of Old Iberia, Admiral of the Armada, Captain of the Stultifera, and King of my own kingdom. You, a mere Inquisitor, dare question me?
...(Shakes head impatiently)
So the church's fools have crossed the ocean to find me. What did you expect, a pat on the back?
Go back. No matter how you got here, the Stultifera Navis does not welcome you. Garcia, my chief mate... no need be so bothered by them.
They will repay the sea a thousand fold for the blood they made you spill.
Ah... (Nods)
Mocking the Inquisition is a serious crime in Iberia.
Did you not understand what I just said? Leave. There is not a single living soul on this ship, child of Old Iberia.
We've sacrificed so much to—!
—I'm not here to ask for your permission!
Such impertinence. This is why I never liked the church.
A crime? Against a church too helpless to stand against even the feeble royal house?
If you're really from Old Iberia, then tell me. Who is the incumbent pope? Carmen?
...You know Saint Carmen's name?
Saint? A saint? Ha...
Haha... A saint... Hahaha.
What are you laughing at?!
He didn't even have the mettle to become pope? Does he not feel ashamed when he sees himself in the mirror?
No wonder it took you sixty years to find this place. It seems disaster has made Iberia weak.
But no matter. Before the ship's doctor joined the corals, we discussed what might have become of Iberia. This is not unexpected.
The floor that you stand on, this immaculate golden hall—THIS is the motherland I know.
THIS is Iberia, not the backwater you speak of, half of which you refuse to see or understand.
How far has the once-great empire fallen? Is it really so pathetic that it took you sixty years to find me?
Alfonso descends from the podium.
Irene takes a step back. Her lantern flickers.
Now then, why are there two Ægir with you? There is no place for the Ægir in my Iberia.
We meant no offense, Captain. We were merely chasing some ugly monsters, and mistook your chief mate for one of them.
...How did you survive so long here?
That's the question, ain't it, you Ægir fools? It's been so, so many years since I took up hunting, as a hobby. Either today or tomorrow should've been the day of reckoning.
But you chased away my quarry. Now we must once again rely on those—
—No. Wait... no!
You... you are not Ægir! You're more than Ægir! Both of you!
What's your name?
...Skadi.
No, no. No no no, Ægir. Don't try to deceive me. We may have been trapped on this ship for decades, but we are no fools.
They call you Ishar-mla.
Did you hear the bells, Ulpianus?
It's a miracle that anything works on this ship at all.
Here, the bells signal the start of the hunt.
But who's the hunter, and who's the prey?
The children of the ocean don't care. If they consider Alfonso to be their kin—albeit weakened—they're willing to offer their own flesh and blood, all to ensure the survival of the strong.
A Hunter approaches.
Gladiia. She's too sharp. The skills I learned on land will only work for so long. She'll detect me soon.
What will you do?
What will YOU do?
Answering a question with a question? I... I'm interested in your choice, Ulpianus.
I never expected to become interested in the Abyssal Hunters again, but Laurentina fascinates me. I see so much possibility in her.
And you, you've made your choice of your own accord, while retaining your sanity. Have you noticed? More and more, you're becoming like one of us.
Can you reach the truth? Cast aside revelation, lay open the body of god, and learn the truth of the gods of the sea?
The answer you're seeking is tantalizing. Heh, look at me, showing curiosity. Perhaps there's something human in me yet.
The revelation is imprinted upon the instincts of god's children. Our ways of thinking couldn't be more different.
Oh my.
You sound even more like a Seaborn than I do.
Ah, you've returned.
Thank you for your contribution. I know how painful it must be for someone like you to go against your faith.
But providence lies just ahead. This is by no means a false promise, but true redemption.
I thank you.
Lord Carmen is far beyond our comprehension. There's no point trying to deceive him. He will discover the truth before long.
You should also thank Thiago. Our lie has condemned him to death at the hands of the Inquisition.
...I have never seen the Herald of the Sea, but rumor has it, the Herald will not refuse the requests of its kin.
Because the choices of kin must surely benefit the entire kind, the thoughts of kin must also be shared by the entire kind. We ought to learn from this, as this is also a part of the Cycle.
Then we'll let Thiago worry about himself.
But...
Ah, you still have doubts. They are unnecessary.
Our prophet, Miss Amaia. Will she really be able to come back?
She has simply chosen to return to the sea one step ahead of us. She is to locate our path, our Herald, and the trails of the sleeping city.
If she returns, it is to help her kin. If she does not, it is because she is making preparations.
We needn't worry too much about her. Just know that she wishes to avoid the scrutiny of the Inquisition, regardless of where she might be.
...Then this person, this Iberian. Why did we abduct him?
His patroness seems to have made some kind of agreement with Carmen. He is an enemy.
Haha... I really wish you'd choose another way of describing my relationship with the doctor.
You've been investigating us, have you not?
I've devoted quite a lot of time explaining our ideals to you, and we would prefer not to hurt you. Have you thought things through?
I think I've heard everything I need to know about your hypocrisy.
...Hypocrisy? Ah, you still speak of morality, the morality of the human realm.
I couldn't care less what these Sea Terrors or Seaborn really are. All I see are a bunch of outlaws inventing a reason to vent their frustrations with Iberia and the Inquisition.
While I haven't lived here for a particularly long time, I AM an Iberian.
And I've been to a lot of places and seen a lot of different things. People like you who think they're special are actually a dime a dozen.
If the gods you talk about are real, and, uh, all that nonsense about being the pinnacle of evolution or the bleeding edge of life—
—Then surely those gods must look down on people like you, right?
How dare you!
...No, he's right. That is why the Herald no longer speaks to us. That is why only Amaia can hear the voice of the ocean, and only she can guide us.
(So there really was something off about Amaia. Tch, I should've caught on sooner.)
(That said...)
How's it looking?
Whatever those things of yours are, that nethersea brand, they aren't able to get into the center of town at all.
That black monster can do more than fly, it can even belch out terrifying jets of flame. The nethersea brand can't spread faster than it's being burned.
If this gets dragged out, then Gran Faro will fall into the Inquisition's hands as soon as the Sea Terrors holding back the Penal Battalion are exterminated.
Then we all die.
No, Thiago, no.
All these things you speak of... You do not grieve for the kin who willingly give themselves up to hold back our enemies.
In other words, you are not yet resolved to sacrifice yourself for our entire kind at a moment's notice.
Don't talk to me like that. Our interests happen to line up this time. I'm just an old fogey with no interest in becoming an ocean-hugger.
...What a lowborn, stubborn creature.
Same to you, friend.
And you, lad, didn't I warn you not to meddle in other peoples' affairs?
Thiago, you—
You know each other?
Ugh...
No. He's just an outsider who kept hanging around the chapel. As the mayor, I need to have at least some idea of every person in town.
You should've stayed out of it.
Right then.
We should act soon, right? Or are we going to wait for the Inquisitor and his monster-wielding accomplice to burn my town to the ground?
We will not. You seem awfully attached to this city. Why?
Gran Faro is the embodiment of our generation-spanning vision to rebuild our country, heretic. Ask any of the Iberians who've joined you, and you'll understand.
The Inquisition has taken enough from us, and I intend to pay them back double.
I am Skadi, not Ishar'mla.
You pronounce it differently, but what does it mean?
Skadi knows, but dares not answer.
Gladiia told her that she is alright, and she is willing to believe her.
But that is only an escape. She knows what the name means.
It means a thunderous roar within the abyss. Flesh and bone rubbing against the rock walls until only the Will remains, surging beyond the ocean.
It means that after that harrowing battle, something was left in her body. 'Something.'
My blood and your blood are identical.
You smell my scent, exactly as we smell your scent.
You find us, kill us...
When we do not understand, we kill you too.
We feed the ocean. Our bodies have provided for the ocean.
Ishar-mla, we possess the same home.
Inquisitor! How dare you join forces with them!
W-What?
Get off my ship this instant! Otherwise you'll join them in my hunt, as the quarry!
Easy there, Mister Captain.
Now tell me, how did you... come to learn of this name?
